🧪 Cat ankles!
Clouded leopards and margays are the only two types of cats that can climb headfirst down trees, owing to the unique flexibility of their ankles (tarsi). 
The tarsi of these arboreal predators rotate around ~180° so that their hind feet can grip even when they are face down/ass up.

Prairie dogs are a keystone species, meaning a lot of animal and plant species depend on them for survival. They're a food source for many different predators, their burrows provide homes for other animals, and their eating habits promote healthy plant diversity

I just read the loveliest theory that cats sniff your eyes because they want to check your pheromones (like  in their scent glands) to understand how you’re feeling. 

It’s usually followed by bunting, so definitely affectionate. Ours do it a lot, but only to me.

I can’t stop thinking about it😻💕🥹

How to get a semi-chill dog during over an hour of rolling thunder: plug in thunder-ease calming pheromone diffuser, close shades so he can't see the lightening, and blast 80s music to drown out the sound.

Don't knuckle amputate your cats. (It shouldn't have a cutesy name like "declawing") It causes horrible agonizing pain and lifelong disability. It can even cause bone shards to stick out of your cat's paws underneath the surface, with no symptoms except horrible agonizing pain. (1/2)

Cats are not like humans, who have sex all the time for pleasure because ovulation is hidden. They do not have "love lives." The female has sex only during estrus, & it is painful.

But they do have friendships, & those friendships are actually facilitated by spaying & neutering.

We had a big pet health scare over the weekend where Mojo, who suddenly lost a bunch of weight, was diagnosed with Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP). A few years ago it was 100% fatal, but it turns out that while remdesivir is not great at treating COVID, it can save your cat's life.

Two researchers began studying cats in 2021 to learn more about how they communicate and express themselves. 

After videotaping and reviewing felines’ facial expressions at a cat cafe for nearly a year, they found that cats displayed at least 276 different facial expressions.

Fun fact: Black and white spotted cats can also be solid black or solid white, simply without visible spotting. It's based on the KIT gene. 

The solid color is just cosmetic, because genetically they're the same as cow kitties or tuxedos.

African wild dogs work cooperatively - and democratically - to hunt. When one dog decides it's time to hunt, it sneezes. If most pack members also sneeze (agreeing), it's time!

Zoo Boise's brother pack - Cassius, Dax, and Teddy - sure isn't anything to sneeze at!

📷: Zoo volunteer Karla

It may be BAD reasons. They might try to bite every human due to illness, or trauma or deliberate training. 

They might fear bite. They might bully bite (anything weaker). 

But a dog that ONLY bites secret service agents?

Dogs are not obligate carnivores. Also, animals do not have the capacity to associate the effects of their diet with their owner so that's not really a test of whether a diet is good or not. My cats would worship me if I fed them only treats, that doesn't mean it's good for them.

Did you know that when given the choice, many cats will gravitate to 10-15 small meals a day? The SureFeed microchip feeder lets you see how many times a day your cat visits the feeder and how much they eat! Ruby is a true grazer.

So, an interesting property of cats: Uncovering danger

Cats seem to understand that their prior ecological niche was non-apex predator and as such, every interaction they enter, they're constantly reevaluating danger. No matter how cool they are with something, they're prepared for it to all go bad

The cat had a favourite armchair to snooze on. 

Because she sheds incessantly I put her favourite blanket on the chair.

She stopped using the chair.

Eventually I took the hairy cat-blanket off so I could use the chair, seeing as the cat no longer liked it.

The cat is snoozing on the chair.

My cat drools when he's really relaxed and the first time it happened I was a little freaked out because I'd lived with cats most of my life and never encountered that. Now when he drools on me I'm just like, yeah, little guy, I glad I'm taking good care of you and making you happy.

My friends have two Maine Coon. One is still technically a kitten but the other is probably fully grown by now. They are lovely cats and very friendly. They even allow for belly scratches without trying to bite or scratch like most cats do. Good luck trying to pick them up and carry though.

When the cat was ill, we experimented with different locations for her food and water. Now the water bowl is by our bedroom door. She drinks when she walks into the room, and also drinks ostentatiously when I am trying to sleep and she wants to annoy me. She's been drinking a lot of water.

The power of a good pastiche, rather than a parody, is that it nails both what is *good* and bad about something, so that the source finds it now can’t escape seeming to reference the pastiche. Listening to Pixies’ Trompe Le Monde, which now sounds like a tribute to Chris Morris’ Motherbanger.

Jazz OTD: Perhaps the Blue Note-iest of all Blue Note tracks, Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers' "Moanin'" is from the October 30, 1958 session that became the album of the same name. An unforgettable Bobby Timmons composition, superb hard bop work by Golson, Morgan, Merritt, and Blakey. jazzsky

The first COMPLETE performance of Debussy’s magical Nocturnes took place on 27 October 1901. The extraordinary beauty of this glorious work has been well-captured on disc, and not only by French performers. What are your favourite recordings of Debussy’s Nocturnes? #classicalmusic

Erich Kornhold’s Symphony was premiered - on radio, not live - in a ‘poorly rehearsed’ performance on 17 October 1954. The first concert performance did not take place until Kempe took the work on in 1972. Nowadays, this is a repertoire work. #classicalmusic

wild how you could listen to almost any genre of music in the 1970s - from dirtbag metal to highfalutin prog, from monomaniacally hypnotic disco to the slipperiest funk jam, from stripped-down post-punk to elaborate jazz fusion - and encounter the most bewildering synthesizer noises imaginable

Frank Zappa and Eddie Van Halen are considered legendary... and while I like a lot of their music, it's more what they contributed to the rock lexicon that I admire.  Rock music is much better for the flourishes they added to its language.

Jazz OTD: with his nearly peerless ear for talent, Art Blakey assembled a truly remarkable iteration of the Jazz Messengers (Freddie Hubbard, Wayne Shorter, Curtis Fuller, Cedar Walton, and Reginald Workman) for “Free For All,” recorded February 10, 1964. Jazzsky

Sorry to hear of the death of Betty Davis: Great funk, a real force of nature, and raunchy as hell. She's the Mademoiselle Mabry on Miles Davis's Filles de Kilimanjaro album, and we wouldn't have the title Bitches Brew without her.

Though there's no shortage of great works in Bartók's oeuvre, his string quartets are surely among the finest music composed in the 20th century. Emotionally and intellectually exhausting, hypnotic, crisp & bold. If they don't send shivers down your spine, please do check your pulse.

jazzsky

Morphine just might’ve been the best 90s band. The perfect intersection of rock and jazz, dark and sexy, danger and mystery. A singular sounding band. I’m never not in the mood to listen to these guys.

Today’s album is Bruce Springsteen’s Born to Run, in my opinion the greatest album of all time. I’m listening to it as it was meant to be heard: on vinyl.

Born to Run is a “four corners” record. See, when we listened to music on physical media with two sides, it gave artists another dimension.

It's very much an Out of the Blue kind of night. A solid 20 track greatest hits - but it's not - it's just a studio album. Includes the epic four track Concierto for a Rainy Day, of which Mr Blue Sky is just one small part, as well as so many non-single album tracks that would slay in their own rigt

Witnessing Janelle Monaé’s evolution from a Philip K. Dick inspired dystopian afrofuturist to an artist who works almost exclusively in the present as a vessel of body positive queer Black joy and realizing it’s all a purposeful act of revolutionary sonic warfare against our oppressive status quo

34 years ago today, on November 2, 1989, Nirvana started their first European tour as the support act for TAD. 

This was 2 years before Nirvana took over the world, changing the course of music and what’s possible in the industry, opening up the doors and windows for other DIY and outsider acts.

One last record and this one needs no introduction. It’s a classic, arguably Kraftwerk’s best, and a landmark of electronic music.

This is the German version and I found it at a used record store in Helsinki on my first trip to Finland. 

It has a pack-in poster too, but I’m not taking that out.

Gothtober reaches the end of Bauhaus with their fourth album, Burning From The Inside. Ned Raggett, writing for The Quietus in 2013, called the album "a compelling sprawl at its best, a case for fragmentation as beauty".

I know playing "the first of" anything is a mug's game, but this has to be one of the first fully formed shoegaze LPs. Kinda amazing it came out while SST quality control was in free fall. Pretty rare as a result.

seeing Dylan's annual number of shows broken down like this is fascinating (on Expecting Rain); the relatively modest pace of the '60s through 1978, when suddenly he goes full-tilt; the exhausting pace of his turn-of the-century touring

I agree, poster: the selection of Bob Rock as producer for the black album was a deliberate attempt to produce a more commercially appealing record that abandoned the band's less radio-friendly thrash metal sound in favour of a more polished, hard rock one.

It’s amazing how Shuggie Otis’ original of “Strawberry Letter 23” is better than the Brothers Johnson cover, except when you’re listening to the Brothers Johnson cover of “Strawberry Letter 23” and it’s better than the original.

ELO and Slade are local bands by the way, from my hometown, but both epic in their own rights. I actually look at Slade as a precursor to screamo, weirdly. The way Noddy shifts between softly worded ballad voice to husky, Walsallian growl, pre-empts bands like Linkin Park essentially doing the same.

Paul McCartney was 24 when he wrote Eleanor Rigby. That’s one of those facts that should break the brains of the performative ‘The Beatles were shit’ people. He was preternaturally talented and no amount of Frog Choruses could undo that.

Talking Heads is an all-time great band.

How good was their rhythm section? Well, their bassist and drummer had a side project called Tom Tom Club, which had a little song in 1981 called Genius of Love. If you haven’t heard it before, yes you have.

Jazz OTD: Throughout the 1950s, Sonny Rollins seemed eager to bring a jazz edge to whatever sources he could, and on his brilliant Blue Note debut, "Sonny Rollins Vol. 1" (recorded December 16, 1956), he and Wynton Kelly have fun with "How Are Things in Glocca Mora" from "Finian's Rainbow." jazzsky

i figure a lot of people who follow me are involved in the entertainment industry so i assume a lot of you know what one is, but if you dont: the ABBA album and song “Super Trouper” are named after a kind of follow spotlight!

Jazz OTD: Reportedly strung-out, underrehearsed, and on a horn in need of repair, alto saxophonist Art Pepper sat down on January 19, 1957 for a session with three members of Miles Davis's first great quintet. And voila! Virtually a consensus pick as one of the greatest jazz albums of all. jazzsky

do yourself a favor and listen to stevie wonder introducing a london nightclub to "signed, sealed, delivered" before its release in 1970

the performance is astounding, from a live album that was exclusive to the UK until it was digitally released the 2000s

Jazz OTD: On January 16, 1958, Ahmad Jamal recorded his now-beloved "But Not For Me -- Live at the Pershing," which went on to become one of the best-selling jazz piano trio albums of all. This is the album's unlikely hit single, showcasing Jamal's exquisite sense of timing and space. jazzsky

it makes perfect sense not liking noise music, but when i dig into somebody like kevin drumm or aaron dilloway all their work is so different and pokes my brain in a bunch of different parts and it has really taught me a lot about textures and frequencies i would not find 'nice' but turns out i like

"What is Bubblegrunge?"

Bubblegrunge was once a real term, usually derisive, referring to poppier bands that found success in Nirvana's wake. Then nobody used it for 20 years, and then in 2020 Spotify unilaterally decided to use the term for female fronted indie pop

If you like classical music than you really need to listen to Aram Khachaturian's symphonies. They're all on YouTube or whatever. Holy wow, I wish he had written more. The third just gets progressively more nuts toward the end. All three are ridiculously impressive.

i feel like an old man as im having physical reactions to beatles discourse lmfao obv musical taste is subjective, but as a slight audio recording and production nerd, their use of the studio as an instrument was revolutionary and you could make the argument that they pioneered multitrack recording

There’s an alternate universe where Beck didn’t randomly meet a hip-hop producer and record “Loser”; in that universe, he keeps playing acoustic stuff, takes a while to find an audience, and has a career that greatly resembles John Darnielle’s

Stevie Wonder - Songs in the Key of Life. 1977 album of the year! Rolling Stones 4th best album of all time! A masterwork of R&B, soul, avant-pop, and progressive soul. This was the culmination of Wonder's classic period. A to Z Vinyl summer spins towards the end!

Jazz OTD: The brilliant album “Milestones” marked, appropriately, a pivotal moment in jazz history, with the end of Miles Davis’s first great quintet. Cannonball Adderley made it a sextet, and Red Garland walked out after recording four tracks, including this one, on February 4, 1958. Jazzsky

this video of DEVO from 1979 shows them at peak form. they play fast, they play tight, and they play hard.

if you don’t know how insane of a live band DEVO was at their peak, this is a good way to find out

This beautiful soul is Rick Wakeman, who you probably know from Yes, he's a bit of a legendary organ player.

But he was also a session pianist and you'll find him in surprising places: Morning has Broken by Youssef Cat Stevens, Hey Jude by The Beatles, and Life on Mars by Bowie being the biggies...

#MusicHistory #OTD 1977 the #SexPistols released their debut, one & only, definitive punk album, Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols (released 11/11 in the US). English visual artist Jamie Reid created the Day-Glo "red & yellow" for the UK cover, while the US version was "pink & green".

Gothtober Vinyl Party rolls on with Twitch by Ministry. Their 2nd album has a more aggressive sound, influenced by industrial dance groups Cabaret Voltaire & Front 242. Robert Christgau has described Twitch as "fleetingly gothic, marginally industrial, and unrelenting in a vaguely threatening way."

It’s really buck wild the myth that Ringo couldn’t play drums lasted as the received wisdom for as long as it did when there are so many sessions out there where he is fucking pounding the drums. Anyway, call me when you were the piece the put the Beatles over from great bar band to superstars.

i feel like i need to remind people that arguably the best celine dion song, IT’S ALL COMING BACK TO ME, was originally written for Meat Loaf and once you know that, 1) you can’t unhear it and 2) it makes the song even better

Insane that refused released the album the shape of punk to come, which was a riff on ornette colemans the shape of jazz to come, and both albums were landmarks nobody was ever able to imitate or replicate

RS500: 408 Motörhead is def out of my comfort zone. My aggro music is more punk than metal. But there’s actually a punk edge to this that made it pretty fun musically. Too bad the lyrics are so dumb. I bet the release of this 4 years before Spinal Tap isn’t a coincidence. Fave song: Ace of Spades

It kind of blows my mind that this house - this house! - is where John Coltrane wrote A Love Supreme and Alice Coltrane recorded Monastic Trio and Journey in Satchidananda. The suburbs hold many secrets, y'all.

Jazz OTD: I don't know the dates for individual tracks, but on December 4-5, 1957, Miles Davis recorded the evocative, misty soundtrack to Louis Malle's "Ascenseur pour l'échafaud." Honestly, this beautiful record might be the most distinctive-sounding jazz album of all. jazzsky

Jazz OTD: Cannonball Adderley’s leadership of the “Somethin’ Else” session on March 9, 1958 is a bit nuanced, given that the lead is often taken by his overqualified sideman, Miles Davis. But holy cow does he own this solo. A universally beloved album, deservedly so. jazzsky

The big thing that hit me when I first listened to Joy Division was that the singer was not trying to be "cool" in any way. Not talking about girls or writing slick poetry but just speaking directly about his pain

Another piece first heard over the radio, Stravinsky’s wondrous Violin Concerto was premiered on 23 October 1931. This is an immediately engaging work, full of magical wit and humour. What are your favourite performances?

RS500/443 Second tier Bowie is still Bowie so no complaints about an excuse to listen to Scary Monsters. I feel you can hear him trying to anticipate (or invent) what rock will be in the 1980s. It doesn't always work but when it does, it's impressive. Fave songs: Ashes To Ashes; Teenage Wildlife

Bohemian Rhapsody. We Will Rock You. Somebody To Love. All hit singles, and all the direct product of a band that was formed when an astrophysicist and a dentistry major found a new friend in an art college, who then went on to recruit a fourth member from the electronics school.

50 Greatest Prog Rock Albums. No. 37. King Crimson: Discipline. 1981. First of three spectacular 80s KC albums, a big change in the sound here, but that's what KC is all about, new sounds, new directions, new experiments.

King Crimson | Elephant Talk 

These four guys together? Ridiculously good!
And bow down to the incomparable Adrian Belew, just oozing pure joy. 🙌

Saw him last year on the Remain in Light tour, and yes, he remains every bit as enthusiastic & energetic. 
Live music doesn't get much better than this.

At Last! By Etta James. Rhythm and Blues. Two musics for the price of one! #191 on the Rolling Stones top 500 albums. I personally think it's low, but that whole list is a bit weird. Still, this is a fantastic way to start a Friday night.

Listening to goofy old Trompe Le Monde by The Pixies for the first time in forever. Reminded that

This is what albums should be. Not just a collection of great songs but one that's both varied and coherent

Imagine how amazing it must feel to be the person playing clarinet at the beginning of "Rhapsody in Blue."

Or to be the saxophone player in "Baker Street," ha ha.

Seriously, what a rush that must be.

955/1000 The Stranglers, "Peaches" - Punk sought to strip rock to its essence, which implied 3 chords played on guitars, bass, & drums. Keyboards were supposed to be for long-winded progressive rock weenies, but the Stranglers bravely ignored that and smuggled some funk into punk. #brodystoprock1000

Probably my favorite part of the fusion era of Miles Davis is when a song has gotten into a funky groove for several minutes at the beginning and just when you almost forget you haven’t heard any trumpet yet, Miles steps in at the exact perfect moment.

There will never be another Beatles. You know why? Cause the Beatles could only exist in a vacuum. A world that has the Beatles in it can't have another one because part of the existential essence of the Beatles is that no one else had ever done what they were doing.

Jazz OTD: It’s the 60th anniversary of one of the greatest recording sessions in jazz history. On February 25, 1964, Eric Dolphy recorded his album “Out to Lunch” with Freddie Hubbard, Bobby Hutcherson, Richard Davis, and Anthony (later just Tony) Williams. Timeless and essential. Jazzsky

Piranesi was really good but extremely short, if nothing else I appreciate that it understands what it means to "rescue" someone lost in that way. I also imagine the literary allusions play well on people really into the classics. For me it was mostly a clever little book.

This really is a roller coaster of a read. What at first appears to be a light-hearted tale of 1930s bohemian London goes on to encompass illness, destitution and tragic loss, but still finds room for comedy. A gem of a novel. My first by Barbara Comyns but not my last.

Semi-recently read Poe's PYM and Mat Johnson's PYM back to back and I highly recommend this, the experience is absolutely hilarious. I was going to write an essay about it but frankly, Johnson's book kind of incorporates that essay already. If I do write about this, it's gonna be about shoggoths tho

The Saint of Bright Doors reads like a novel-length short story in the best way possible: full of fresh ideas, fast-moving, with an evident care applied to each and every sentence. Remarkable and highly-recommended.

Recommended read: The Blue, Beautiful World by Karen Lord. Stellar example of SF exploring the human condition with insight and compassion in a dramatic tale of ordinary people trying to cope when their small planet is caught between powerful alien races who may decide humanity’s fate is irrelevant.

New blog post: on Emily Tesh's impressive first novel Some Desperate Glory, which pokes at some popular recent tropes in both YA and space opera, and barrels through a whole trilogy's worth of story in a single novel in an extremely satisfying way.

I just finished Mariana Enriquez's Things We Lost in the Fire and damn! I don't know if I've ever read a short story collection with more affecting pacing. The stories are staggered so you never know how far the Horror will creep next time.

Since I’ve been having some lovely conversations about American folk horror, today’s book recommendation is Maynard’s House by Herman Raucher (1980).  A Vietnam vet with PTSD “inherits” a house from a fallen soldier in a remote area of Maine and decides to hole up there for the winter

If you see this, post a book you’ve read more than once.

Spare, bleak, and brutal, WE WHO ARE ABOUT TO… demolishes many of the village pieties of genre SF. It’s also very funny. 

It was originally published in GALAXY during the editorship of Jim Baen. Which is to say, people are complicated.

I finished Jaime Hernandez's MAGGIE THE MECHANIC, and it wasn't at all what I expected but still a pleasure. It's honestly kind of shocking how little friction there is, in terms of Hernandez finding his footing; it's perfectly established, immensely assured but never "showy."

The greatest living ghost story writer is surely Reggie Oliver, who not only writes well, but also in a variety of styles including pastiches. He’s quite remarkable. Much of his work can be purchased through Tartarus Press.

Huge congratulations to Irish author Paul Lynch, who has been awarded the 2023 Booker Prize for his novel Prophet Song, a book set in a dystopic Ireland descending into authoritarian rule. Wonderful news! You can listen to Paul read from the winning novel at Kennys, here: youtu.be/nTNdiITpFqU?...

I’ve been reading Discworld for the first time this year and I cannot overstate how good it is. It’s funny, smart, and has just layers of “oh that’s a good point…” that makes you think and hopefully grow.

I wish I had read them sooner.

Reading Skin Thief by Suzan Palumbo.  Propagating Peonies might be the best story about the deep ambivalence at the heart of Buddhist practice I’ve ever read. This book is so ridiculously good. Deeply embodied stories about the spiritual homelessness of being pulled between 2 worlds

Really enjoying Witches Abroad so far. Love how the witches play off of each other, and also love what the story does with fairy tales. Actually wonder if Shrek took any inspiration from it. Also very much feels like Pterry visited New Orleans once and was like. Ah yes. I know this place. Uh. GUMBO

THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS gets a shoutout from the Chicago Review of Books! 🔥 “Quietly masterful on a prose level … repeatedly shocked me with the cleverness and significance of its narrative twists … One of the most satisfying novels, in any genre, that I’ve encountered in quite some time.”

💙📚 2024-7

Winner of the 2023 Booker, deserves all the accolades. I'm not sure I've ever read a book that affected my physically like Paul Lynch's Profit Song. I had to put it down at times. I had to remember to breathe. The anxiety-inducing story of Ireland's fall into fascism is simply brilliant.

I am currently reading Anna Smith-Spark's A Sword of Bronze and Ashes. It is a mind-blowing book. I don't think I've ever read anything quite like it. it's a story about the ragged, tearing wound you get when high myth gets stitched to the grind of mundane life. It's incredible.

Weekly book talk for Saturday, February 4. What’s everyone reading?

If I had the power to compel 330 million Americans to read one and only one book, it would be The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965).

Am reading The Little Rebel, by French author Jérôme Leroy and it is amazing. The story of tensions and terror in a French city, it’s immensely powerful and thought-provoking. It’s only 80 pages and we’ll worth a read.

I've read a lot of Matheson that I haven't liked, but the reputation I AM LEGEND has of being one of the best horror novels, and one of the best science fiction novels, and according to some one of the best novels of any kind, is completely deserved. A once-in-a-lifetime idea, perfectly executed.

I was always going to read Rachel Nuwer's MDMA book 
'I feel Love', but it really has exceeded my expectations. It's fascinating and informative, not only on the history and current state of psychedelic science as a whole, but on the nature of trauma. Warmly recommended.

He Who Drowned The World by @@166678 is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

PINK SLIME by Fernanda Trías (Heather Cleary transl.) is a sombre ecological catastrophe novel. The narrator lives in a coastal city infested with a toxic form of algae. The fish have died, the rich have moved away, and the protagonist suffers through isolation, depression and a family drama.

I’m shifting my 2023 reading thread from Twitter with #50, which is Room Temperature by Nicholson Baker. Baker is the apotheosis of James’s writer “on whom nothing is lost” and he turns what should be a curse of King Midas proportions (Remember and be forced to share every detail!) into a blessing.

Today's book recommendation is Patrick Modiano's DORA BRUDER, a book about being haunted, and how an experience of haunting leads to obsession. It is about the doomed effort to salvage the pieces of a human life from the genocidal wreckage of history. Above all else, it is a book about loss.

Today's book rec is Paul Beatty's THE SELLOUT, which possesses in great measure what all powerful satires possess: beneath its comedic madcap zaniness there is a scratching, a tearing; an overwhelming sense of the terror of history bubbling up from below; a profound and wholly justified cosmic rage.

The Case of Cem by Bulgarian writer Vera Mutafchieva deserves to be part of the 20th century canon of global literature. Now, thanks to International Booker winner Angela Rodel, Anglophone readers can discover this epic novel. These are finished copies. Let me know if you want an ARC.

A person of great taste (who I can’t recall!) gave me this book a while ago and I’ve only just read it. A work of genius that forever changes how you see Jane Eyre. I can’t remember last time I felt so much warmth towards the main character Antoinette and admiration for a secondary one Christophine.

One thing that a horror writer can do to rapidly seduce me as a reader is to give me the benefit of the doubt. Read any story by John Langan or Gemma Files or Michael Cisco - they assume that I'm paying attention and are not going to "spoon-feed" me.

Re-reading the Tiffany Aching series by Terry Pratchett 💙📚

Highly recommended for any young person (or young at heart!). Like most Pratchett, you can't step without tripping over a nugget of wisdom wrapped in a veneer of humor. 

 I am sure I will be posting quotes all week...

Finished @@166678's HE WHO DROWNED THE WORLD, masterful sequel to SHE WHO BECAME THE SUN. Heartrending, tense, and yet surprisingly hopeful, it asks the question of how far one would go for power and whether it's worth it.

i read 3 james m cain novels last night bc i couldn't sleep and i tell ya the famousest ones (double indemnity, the postman always rings twice) are way less good than mildred pierce, which is Very Very Very Good Indeed

Finished reading The Bloody Chamber by Angela Carter last night. It was fantastic! Thanks to the kind soul who helped me sort out a digital copy. Any fans of this book? Any favorite stories? Loved the takes on Bluebeard and Puss in Boots

Absolutely blown away by C. J. Leede's MAEVE FLY, which sets itself higher and higher hurdles and then backflips over every one of them. It invites so many comparisons veteran authors would quake at, but it earns them. Mean, nasty, solipsistic, amazing.

Picked up Alexievich’s elegant, brutal, heart-breaking, and image-filled history, told as interwoven oral histories, of Soviet women who served in WWII, from laundresses to snipers, and did not put it down until I finished.

My favourite books of the year! The Adventures of Amina Al-Sirafi, by Shannon Chakraborty - fabulous piratical fantasy yarn, Amina is a wonderful narrator who wants only to serve God but finds this sometimes inconsistent with being a pirate, I love it

Stephen King had a birthday!

I read so many books this year. My favorites:

-Number Go Up by Zeke Faux, about the wild world of crypto scams
-Under the Skin by Linda Villarosa, about racism's toll on health
-Some Desperate Glory by Emily Tesh, a sci fi story that is just so brilliant and hard to describe

What were yours?

My third book of the year was Redshirts, by John Scalzi. It's a classic parody, but also critique of writers and the people who make money on them, an existential tome of philosophy, and an examination of interpersonal relationships.

Lunchtime reading! I'm the generation to whom Calvin & Hobbes meant everything, so I was excited by the Mysteries' existence and relieved when I read it. Waterson could have become anything in 28 years. This gorgeous serenely nihilistic fable for adults shows the irritable moral compass remains true

reading Manhunt this evening by Gretchen Felker-Martin, & one thing that strikes me is how beautiful everything is. The way she describes the settings, the world, the fog, the mist, the burned out buildings. There are tender moments, & beautiful, & when combined w the violence & terror it's dizzying

Maggie Nelson’s memoir, The Argonauts, encompassing her transitioning partner and meditations on queer motherhood is maybe one of the best things I’ve read. I’ll be teaching it next semester in the second half of my queer memoir course

Excellent book. Posits the rise of ISIS not as a genuine upheaval of devout religiosity but rather as another strand of right-wing populism that attacks the intellectual class and offers a sense of action, however deeply evil, in a liberal world that seems paralyzed by inaction.

I’ve spent the evening with Essex Hemphill’s 1992 poetry and prose collection, Ceremonies. Dealing with black masculinity and queerness, the AIDS epidemic, kink and sex, and essays on the problem of Robert Mapplethorpe and the joy of Paris is Burning. Highly recommend

Hallucinations by Oliver Sacks is the best book I have read on the subject. Informative. Empathetic. Scientific but an easy read. I have nothing but respect for this man’s work. Hallucinating is a fascinating part of the human experience.

BOOK REVIEW: 

If you have even a passing interest in pre and postcontact Native American warfare and the general idea that militaries are deeply reflective of the cultures and societies that create them I cannot recommend this book enough

Having slept on it I'm confident saying that The Malazan Book of the Fallen is the best fantasy series I have ever read. Completely engrossed me from start to finish. I read ten books, 1200 pages a pop, in four months. I could not put them down. The easiest recommendation I've ever given. Read 'em!

Starling House by Alix E. Harrow.

A gorgeous gothic novel with a haunted house at its center and metatextual footnotes. I love how this story explores how history is created and our understanding of the past, how the stories we tell become what's true.

Read (listened to) 'Lost Hearts' by M.R. James this morning. What a dark, grim, macabre story. If you have the mistaken idea that James is musty or staid, this one will sort you out.

gutenberg.ca/ebooks/james...

Finished Solenoid by Mircea Cartarescu, tr Sean Cotter. A battle at times, but worth the effort I think. A richly imaginative stream/waterslide of consciousness slipping between dream states and reality for a nobody Romanian school teacher. Lots of great lines and tough paragraphs.

Wonderful Sunday reading: this Mexican novel, a fragmentary non-chronological Bildungsroman based on Yukio Mishima’s travels to Mexico and the US as well as his formation as a writer. One of the best Mexican books I’ve read recently. I hope it makes it to translation

With the caveat that I've been reading and enjoying them, I have to say there is some tension in Victoria Goddard's The Hands of the Emperor and At the Feet of the Sun because they are trying to be cozy fantasy empire books and the cozy part is always going to be at odds with the fantasy empire part

I'm currently reading The Doors Of Eden by @@9959, and I have to tell you that it rotates chapters between cryptid hunting, secret service shenanigans, and alternate evolutionary histories with hundred-kilometer long space-faring arthropods. This is what I signed up for :)

A bit late to the party on this one - but absolutely loved Drunk on All Your Strange New Words. Compulsive reading - I've found myself sneaking chapters in between marking papers! Firm recommend for a near-future mystery about translation and the value of truth.

First book read in the new year is "Hi Honey, I'm Homo" by @@792 

If you're familiar with Matt's youtube videos (and you should be) this is the same well-researched, well laid-out, incisive history of the intersection between the gay rights and media representation. Recommended.

Well, just powered to the end of Fiona McFarlane’s *The Sun Walks Down*. One of the more exceptional novels I've read in a while. Beautiful writing and genuinely acute insight into characters … and also into the Australian desert landscapes … in a tense, missing child plot. Major recommendation.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. Her haunting work grapples with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the legacy of the Holocaust, drawn in a style that feels like a fresh amalgam of Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz.

Brilliant short crime stories by @@177147

4 bald eagles (1 adult & 3 juvies, perhaps the triplets born last year on the lake) trying to hunt a cormorant or kleptoparasitize it, aka harassing it until it gives up fish that it caught - a strategy often used by eagles since they are big enough to bully other animals to steal food! #SciComm 🧪🦑🦈

A Sparrowhawk is probably one of the most exciting birds you might see in a garden. Thanks to John Lauper for sharing his sighting.

Female Sparrowhawks are dark brown in colour and larger than the males, who have slate-grey backs.

I've been fascinated with 🪶 Boat-billed Herons as long as I've known they existed, it was a privilege to observe this mating pair discussing suitable nesting sites this morning in Tortuguero National Park, Costa Rica

"In a spectacular sight, flocks of various species of water birds migrating from different parts of Eurasia have started arriving at Point Calimere Wildlife and Bird Sanctuary in Nagapattinam district."

There are also white-throated dippers on the Pader.  The Pader is the shortest river in Germany.  Up to 5000 liters per second flow from around 200 springs and the first mill is operated after 400 m. #birds #scanjack #paderborn

Birdfeeder Drama:

Last week our lone blue-jay learned that mimicking a hawk will cause every squirrel at the feeder to dive for cover.

Just now he tried it on the doves and is visibly perplexed that it didn't fucking work, so he flew down close and tried again.

Then the doves beat his ass.

A Grey Catbird I saw at MacGregor Point PP in Ontario.

Catbirds like to skulk around in the bushes so it was a treat to see one in the relative open. They are related to thrashers and mockingbirds, so as well as their namesake mewing, they have a vast repertoire of elaborate vocalizations.

Let's pretend it's July 23rd instead of January 23rd. Please enjoy this Black Skimmer skimming! They zoom along the surface of the water and catch fish and look so elegant doing it. 

#birds 🌿 #marinelife

From July 2014 and Jan 2015, Long-eared Owls in lean mode and floofy mode. Like most birds they will puff their feathers out to retain heat in the winter, and tuck them in during the summer, making for stark contrasts even though their body weight has barely changed. 🪶

#SuperbOwlSunday #owls

Greater Yellowlegs and its little buddy Lesser Yellowlegs giving a nice rare comparison this evening at Steigerwald Lake in  Washougal, Washington. Greater is not interested in sharing its salamander. #birding

The Gold-ringed Tanager has the local common name Bangsia de Tatama for the very limited range where it is found. The tanager is the symbol of Tatama National Park and a species that has inspired conservation in Colombia. 🪶🌿

Outside of Kazakhstan: Finally we could watch the migration of the Eurasian crane in Kelbra, Germany. It was fantastic to see thousands of birds arriving in dusk at this lake to fly later further to Spain. These birds also living in Kazakhstan and migrating in winter to India.

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

This handsome little scooter is a Cinnamon Flycatcher. They call the Andean cloud forest home and can be found from Venezuela south to Chile. Like most flycatchers you can see them sallying from a perch to catch flying insects. Montezuma Rainforest Lodge, Colombia 🪶🧪🌿

Cormorant comparison. 🪶 The big ones in front are pied cormorants, and the smaller ones are little pied cormorants. They're not often seen together as the little are more land based and the large ones are ocean-going.
Port of Brisbane, QLD #ausbirds

Black-crowned Night-heron is one of the few birds we've seen in Taiwan that *isn't* a lifer. But unlike home, they are amazingly abundant here. Herons, egrets, night-herons, bitterns, all super common and easy to admire. #birding

It has a pinkish-brown body, striking black and white wings, a black downcurved bill, and a pinkish-brown crest which it raises when excited.
   The Eurasian hoopoe is native to Europe, Asia and the northern half of Africa.

Snow petrels live in the most extreme environments. They breed amongst boulders high on Antarctic nunataks where summer temperatures may be -15 Celsius. To find food they have to fly hundreds of kilometres back out to the edge of the sea ice

Parenting of swallows. The swallow's parent bird flies at great speed and shoves a large dragonfly into the chick's mouth. The chicks also try their best to drink the dragonfly. It's powerful.
#birds🪶

Just heard a Carolina Wren make a noise I’ve never heard a Carolina wren make before, kind of a soft trill, almost like a gray tree frog.  If I hadn’t been staring at the bird I would have been utterly baffled. 🪶

Three-cornered leek in the garden and snake's-head fritillary, Chiffchaff and Canada Geese at Lakeside Country Park today. Goldcrest, Firecrest, Reed Bunting, Redwing and Great Crested Grebe were other highlights at Lakeside.

Bird Glamour’s Glamager series continues with Hepatic Tanagers! This southern US to Central American dark red bird loves a pine-oak forest. These birds are little studied, but we do know they eat insects, spiders, and small fruits. Lady Hepatic Tanagers do most of the nest building. 🐡

Bird Glamour loves Halloween colored birds! The Baltimore Oriole loves ripe bright-colored fruit, especially oranges. They also eat many insects we consider pests, like tent caterpillars. Female orioles weave elaborate hanging sock-shaped nests and will recycle old nest materials. 🐡🧪 #BirdGlamour

Mt Laguna is also home to one of my all-time favorite birds: the Acorn Woodpecker! This time they were busy gorging themselves on the abundant insects flying over Little Laguna Lake, unseasonably filled by the tropical storm 🪶

A park ranger in Santee SP in SC once pointed out to me that the round nesting holes in the trees in the middle of Lake Marion are from Pileated Woodpeckers, but the large oval ones were made decades ago by Ivory Billed Woodpeckers, and this fact honestly haunts me - all that's left is a void. 🪶

Black Phoebe. These birds have a long range - from the western US south all the way to Argentina. They are primarily insectivorous. If you love birds, gotta conserve bugs. 🌿🧪🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

How to survive a winter storm: sit on feet, maximize floof, hunker down, take a nap.

Maybe this Mourning Dove is dreaming of warmer weather - finding a nice partner dove and throwing a few sticks together to make a nest. 🪶

Black Phoebes used to be rare to infrequent in Oregon, especially the Northern parts, but now are more and more common. This one graced our presence at Eagle Lookout at Ankeny National Wildlife Refuge today in Marion County, Oregon. #birding

🧪Check out this bald eagle migration in Harrison Mills, Canada 🦅 Each fall, bald eagles flock to the Harrison/Fraser Rivers, drawn by the return of spawning salmon. Kingfishers are known for their looks: stocky body; long, thick bill; and striking colors and markings.
              Found in a variety of habitats on all continents but Antarctica, kingfishers are territorial birds.

Bald Eagle 🦅 

The Bald Eagle embodies grace, power, and adaptability, revered across cultures as a symbol of resilience and connection to the natural world.

They can fly at 20-40 mph but reach up to mind-blowing 100 mph while diving.

Probably the most majestic of them all!

My fav gull! 90% of them nest on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California but a small population began nesting on an island in a small lake and then on the roof of a McDonalds in Seaside, CA near Monterey. The island eroded & McD's burned down, but some friends of mine built a new island for them!

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

A visit to Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge is quickly becoming a yearly ritual. The photos don't really do it justice. The amount of wildlife (mostly birds and alligators) is tremendous and utterly amazing.

i saw bald eagles today. on january first, in canada. they made their gay little chitters (no hawk-like shrieks as USian propaganda would have you believe), sitting on trees, contending with one another, flapping their mighty wings. they are beautiful animals.

Townsend's warblers (Setophaga townsendi) are a migratory visitor to the Gorge in winter. They like to forage & breed high in the treetops of conifers; large-diameter trees are preferred for breeding. For that reason, you won't find them in most urban areas. My yard has ...

We spotted this handsome Barred Fruiteater skulking around and at first thought it was a thrush. These birds are true to their name and only eat fruit. They can be found in the Andes Mountains from Venezuela south to Bolivia. Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

Pink cockatoo
The pink cockatoo, also known as Major Mitchell's cockatoo or Leadbeater's cockatoo, is a medium-sized cockatoo that inhabits arid and semi-arid inland areas across Australia, except the northeast.

Nice mixed flock of mostly White-eyed Parakeets (Psittacara leucophthalmus) with a couple of Red-masked Parakeets (Psittacara erythrogenys) feeding on the fruit of a Bullet Tree (Terminalia buceras), just outside my window 

#MiamiWildlife 🪶

There are some species at that are on the top 10 most wanted birds of many searchers and the Diademed sandpiper-plover is one of those. What a thrill when I finally caught up with this species high in the Andes of #Peru a decade ago. 🪶

🧪Meet the Steller's sea eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus), among the largest of all eagle species by both weight and wingspan! This individual is one half of a mated pair being cared for by Woodland Park Zoo as part of one of the U.S.’s few conservation breeding programs for this species 🦅

I got to see this very photogenic lifer yesterday: a brilliant white Lark Sparrow that was hanging out in a flock of finches and bluebirds, foraging among the dew-covered grass at a local campground. 🪶

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

The flame bowerbird is one of the most brilliantly colored bowerbirds. The male is a medium-sized bird, up to 25 cm long, with flame orange and golden yellow plumage, elongated neck plumes, and a yellow-tipped black tail. It builds an "avenue-type" bower with two side walls of sticks.

Canada jays are quintessential northern birds - omnivorous, with thick fluffy plumage. They don't migrate, and breed in late winter (Feb/March) before arrival of spring migrants to their boreal forest home 🪶

Swallow-tailed Cotinga in the Serra do Mar outside Rio de Janeiro last month - a #bird I hadn't expected to see as bad weather forced me to cancel a planned day of higher altitude birding, so a wonderful surprise when this one turned outside the lodge as I waited out rainstorms. 🪶 #BirdsOfBrazil

Open water on the lakes and ponds around Anchorage #Alaska is getting pretty scarce but Common Goldeneyes can get by with surprisingly little water. A few usually manage to stick it out after most other ducks have bailed on us. 🪶

Honing photography skills picking off wary warblers certainly helps with flighty rainforest birds. This handsome friend is a Common Tody-Flycatcher and it never stopped moving!

A drake Common Goldeneye stretching his wings at @@299642 Saltholme reserve yesterday. Small numbers of them are wintering on the East Saltholme Pool and usually a few may linger until second half of April. #ukbirding #ukwildlife 🪶

Golden Tanagers are native to the cloud forests of South America from Venezuela to Bolivia. They eat a combination of fruit and insects and tend to travel in mixed flocks.

Collared Incas are snazzy little hummingbirds. Most of the time when you see them, they look like they are wearing a black tuxedo. But if you catch them in just the right light, you can see some green to their feathers. 🧪🌿🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

The eared grebe has a piercingly red eye — totally freaky. In the fall almost all of them fly to Mono Lake or Great Salt Lake and pig out on brine shrimp and alkali flies, losing most of their flight ability and doubling in weight. They migrate at night. #Birds

Hi 👋 Sharpie! After a bit of light rain must be a good time to do a little grooming. Keeping up that handsome appearance is hard work. Skinny legs and a dark hood and about the size of a Mourning dove - good tells for a Sharpie.

Endangered California gnatcatcher in the wonderful coastal sage habitat here down by the Mexican border in the Tijuana River Valley open space preserve. In the first shot he is doing his mewing call that sounds like a kitten. 🪶

A fantastic photo to start your week. Taken by my friend Alan McFadyen, who runs local wildlife photography hides. He took the famous pic of a kingfisher diving vertically, just before it entered the water.

The great blue heron is a large wading bird in the heron family Ardeidae, common near the shores of open water and in wetlands over most of North and Central America, as well as far northwestern South America, the Caribbean and the Galápagos Islands

I hear it's Black-crowned night heron day. Count me in. They're such sleek birds. Found on every continent except Australia and Antarctica, they are the most widespread heron species in the world! #Birds

This immature Great Blue is growing up! Its dark crest is coming in nicely, and those long stringy feathers on its chest -- used both for protection from rough reeds and branches, and for intimidation of other herons -- are starting to appear. Lost Lagoon, Stanley Park, August 27 🪶

The black-headed gull is a small gull that breeds in much of the Palearctic including Europe and also in coastal eastern Canada. Most of the population is migratory and winters further south, but some birds reside in the milder westernmost areas of Europe

We were happy to find a pair of Powerful Woodpeckers in Ecuador, where they aren't very common. If you wonder what an Ivory-billed Woodpecker looked like in the wild, the Powerful Woodpecker is in the same genus and looks similar.

The final Bird Glamour Glamager look is the Flame-colored Tanager! This complex-colored tanager calls Mexico & Central America home & sometimes AZ home. They are a mountain bird, shifting their territory to higher elevations due to climate change, but mountains only go so high! 🐡🧪

Still going thru my Alaska pics-- here;s a nice Red Phalarope Female in her bright breeding plumage. Utqiagvik (Barrow) Alaska, the Northhern-most city in North America is a great place to see shorebirds up close. #Birds

Kinshi tamago, also known as thinly shredded egg omelette, is a delightful Japanese culinary art. It involves cooking a thin omelette, then slicing it into delicate, narrow strips. These silky strands add a beautiful touch to various dishes and an interesting textural contrast.

Made the Short Rib Onion Soup from Smitten Kitchen yesterday. Afgan mantu (pronounced mantwo) are usually made with lamb, but pumpkin is a main ingredient in Afghan cuisine and I wanted mine with a version of borani kaddo: heavily spiced chunks of roasted pumpin and onion. The dumplings and sauces (yogurt, turmeric tomato, herby green chile) are plant-based.

Two of my favourite Bangladeshi dishes which evoke memories of childhood - “aloo satni”, potatoes mashed with raw diced onion, fried chillies and garlic, mustard oil, fresh coriander and “aloo bhazi”, finely chipped potatoes stir fried with onion, garlic, chilli, fresh coriander

Orange, green olive, red onion, a sardine, and some chili flakes. Less than €10 at a sit-down restaurant, and one of the best things I ate in Sicily. An object lesson in humbling all preconceptions before the majesty of fresh simple local ingredients.

Tip for New Years Eve, if you’re having caviar, put it with crème fraiche on Ruffles potato chips. Blini is hard to find and I think it’s fuddy duddy. Cheapest kind of caviar is salmon roe. This is domestic paddlefish.

Seafood Doria is a type of gratin that combines seafood, white sauce, cheese. It is made by layering rice, seafood, and sauce in a baking dish, and then baking it until the cheese is melted and bubbly. Seafood Doria is a popular dish in Japan, and is often served hot and enjoyed as a comfort food.

Just saw a sausage gravy recipe that called for pouring off the sausage grease and then making the roux with butter. You do you but I’m just adding flour to that grease because it’s already there, hot, and full of sausage flavor. 🍜🍽️

Sichuan inflected Shrimp Scampi over cabbage.

Two things people do wrong with French toast:

One, use thin bread. Yuck. Should be 3/4” thick, or 1”. 

Two, don’t soak it long enough. I’ll be giving my bread an hour to soak up all the egg. If you soak too short, the inside of the bread gets no egg or flavour. 🍜🍽️

My new favorite way to make potatoes. Get the small variety.

Boil them for 15 minutes (start preheating yoir air fryer after 10).

Drain them and let the water steam off, then toss them in some butter or oil and salt.

Air fry them on 400 for 10 minutes. Toss them halfway through.

Serve and enjoy.

This is how we do coleslaw in Greece! It’s called lahanosalata (cabbage salsa). Finely sliced cabbage and onion heavily salted for an hour to soften, then rinsed and dressed with olive oil and either lemon or wine vinegar. So yummy but watch out later 💨💨😂😂

the best caramelized onions you will ever make:

4-5 onions of various kinds, sliced 

5 tbsp salted butter

In a large saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the onions and mix. Turn heat to low. Cook for 4-5 hours, till onions are browned and sweet, with an almost jam-like texture.

when browning or searing meat be careful not to crowd the pan or the meat will stew! Getting all wet and make the meat tough and unappetizing. start in overly small batches until you are sure your batch size stays dry and cooks the way you want.
