While in Naples today I also could not resist to try Neapolitan Pizza at Re Pazzo -  Pizza E Sfizzi - both dough and ingredients were fantastic so I ate way more than I should have. Pizzas are made at 70% hydration with Marvello flour - can’t recommend enough 🍜

Tortilla Española. Made with leftover French fries and (unnecessary) shrimp. Why is this so good? It has no business being this good. It's a stupid potato and onion omelet. I might make another one tonight. With balcony Sungolds and parsley. 🍽️🌱

once again, i would like to promote the homemade Taco Bell potatoes: create a 50/50 flour and corn starch dredge, season with all your favorite southwest dried spices and salt to taste, toss diced potatoes to coat, toss in cooking oil, convection roast until crispy. no par-boiling needed.

Pickled red onions. Huzzah. It’s a riff on what Bobby Flay always makes — except I’m cheap & I use white vinegar instead of red wine vinegar with grenadine. 

Flay never adds anything past RWV/grenadine but I added a bay leaf, pinch salt/MSG, & several pink peppercorns. Keeps for a few months.🍜🍽️

Inspired by a chef on instagram, I just made rigatoni alla boscaiola, and  it’s off the chain. Porcini, blue oyster and cremini mushrooms, pancetta, shallots, garlic, Italian tomato paste, Calibri peppers, white wine, cream, seasoning on rigatoni, topped w gremolata and pecorino.

If you are someone who likes umami flavor, can I recommend toasted soy sunflower seeds? 

Heat sunflower seeds in skillet until toasted. Turn heat off. Add dashes of soy sauce / tamari to hot pan. Shake rattle roll. 

Add to salads, pasta, wherever you want a salty crunchy kick. Keeps for a while.

We're firing up the barbecue for the first time this year. A perfect excuse to make Ottolenghi's shallow-fried potatoes with rosemary and sumac - they will go perfectly with steak. The recipe is from the Simple cookbook, which I recommend wholeheartedly. 🍜🍽️

Soft, cloud-like sesame milk buns made using the Tangzhong method. Sprinkled b&w white sesame seeds just before going into the oven and finished with a brush of garlic and spring onion in melted butter and flaky sea salt. 

Love the textures, will go well w/ an Asian dinner.

Hot sauce taxonomy:

Yellow: Caribbean; mustard, vinegar, pepper

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha defines the type, but is its most mid member

Cholula-Tapatio: Classic hot sauce, more pepper than vinegar, often with some garlic

Tabasco et al: mostly vinegar

Beliezian: Like Cholula/Tapatio, but with carrot

This is μακαρόνια με κιμά (the Greek version of ragù), spiced with cinnamon, clove and allspice and topped with Parmesan and feta. If you’ve not tired this (and you’re not veg/vegan) you need this in your life 😋🥰🥰

I made a side for a steak dinner on the weekend called potato pavé, very finely sliced potatoes layered in garlic, sage and thyme infused cream, baked low for hours, compressed overnight then sliced and pan fried. It was very good

My bean consumption has been transformed. I've been making chili, curry, etc. from grocery store dry beans for years and never been quite satisfied. These remarkable beans are from Rancho Gordo, so superior to store beans that they are like a completely different material. I will never go back.

It is weird burger hours. The bun is a turmeric brioche with nigella seeds; the burger is This Isn't Beef; the cheese is gruyere; the sauce is okonmoni sauce and kewpie mayo; the pickle is Vietnamese dưa cải chua.  🍽️🍜

How to make a good red sauce:

1. Use the best canned whole tomatoes you can get and crush em with your pawbs
2. Also add in a can of crushed tomatoes for extra depth of flavor
3. Cook your tomato paste!!!
4. Lots of fresh garlic
5. Season throughout cooking not just at the end
6. Use a wooden spoon

Pasta alla gricia is an amazing old school dish in the "dry" class (ie dressed, not sauced). Very simple, it includes only the pasta, guanciale, pepper, water, and Pecorino Romano. It is rich, indulgent, and delicious.

PROTIP: skinning, seeding, and blending up a Honeycrisp apple, then adding it to standard Japanese-style curry made from roux blocks along with a squeeze of honey and a glug of soy sauce, takes the whole affair from pretty good to GOD DAMN.

The 'Ajitama,' a flavorful egg marinated in a Japanese-style dashi and soy sauce, is a popular dish. I used a concentrated stock called 'Shirodashi' and a citrusy soy sauce, 'Ponzu'. Its subtle acidity entices the appetite, and you can savor the depth of the egg flavor with a hint of stock.

My first Moroccan eggs this year! Now that I can eat decent gluten-free bread again, I can revisit some of my fave eggs. :)

(Berber eggs are like shakshuka but a different flavour profile — garlic, ginger, turmeric, chili powder, cumin, oregano, in grated tomatoes and cooked down for 20 min.)🍽️🍜

I'm at my happiest when I'm cooking.

This started as dry aged Porterhouse bones cooked for 15 hours 

Now roasted veggies & herbs are stewing for hours in it.

Tomorrow it becomes French onion soup

(...still looking for where to buy quality dry sherry in this day and age..)

It doesn’t look like much, but this “æbleflæsk” with apples, onions and bacon is a lunch favourite. This is how it’s made in my husband’s family - in my family we would use whole pork strips instead of small pieces of bacon. Maybe this is just a Danish thing?

Home made pork belly sandwich baguette, of course inspired by bahn'mi from Vietnam.
Cabbage, pickles, coriander, bit of chilli, some hotnsauce, and a drizzle of soy sauce, miso, fish sauce mix. Lovely stuff. Made last week with leftover of 2.5 kilo smoked pork belly!

achievements in lunch: you can put a little bit of sticky rice in a mini waffle iron and then dump some soy sauce and furikake on there and plop a soft-boiled egg on top. you can do this and nobody can stop you

Sometimes my brain gets an evil genius voice in telling me to do mad science with food. Inspired by @@1248 , I have some mayo experiment options now that I've figured out stick blender mayo is easy and relatively cheap

What if -
Lime cilantro mayo?
Plum vinegar shiso mayo?

Duck fat fries with garlic dip. (Dip’s a little fresh garlic, chipotle sauce, and 50/50 mayo/Greek yogurt.) 🍜🍽️

Made in the air fryer. Secret is: starchy potato cut thinner, & soaked in cold water, with a rinse. Dry them well before fat/cooking, & brush w more fat 3/4 through cooking them.

How to make Greek style roasted lemon chicken with potatoes!

1. Peel potatoes and mix with juice and zest of 4 lemons, olive oil, a spoonful of mustard, salt and pepper and half a cup of water. Lay on a bed of sliced onions and roast uncovered for 30 min at 200°C.

I rendered the fat from a pound of homemade duck sausage then sautéed (in this order) onions celery garlic parsley and thyme in it. Then added the sausage back in. This will get combined with homemade cornbread for stuffing

My own chili con carne recipe spiced with 4 types of chilli (fresh, aleppo, kashmiri and ancho), oregano, allspice, cinnamon, coffee, cumin, coriander and lime. Sounds random but it works! Served on shredded lettuce and onion dressed in a light vinaigrette cos I’m weird like that 😋😋🥰

Scachatta! The Tampa Bay area is mostly known for Cuban sandwiches, but less so for schchatta. It’s similar to something from Sicily called sfincione, but schchatta has a bunch of egg yolk in the focaccia-like crust, as well as ground meat in the tomato sauce. Eaten at room temperature. Good stuff.

Make lahanosalata (Greek cabbage salad)! Finely slice cabbage, carrot and onion. Mix in 1/4 cup salt. Leave for 2 hours, kneading every 30 min. Rinse twice with water. Squeeze out as much liquid as poss. Store with a  few spoonfuls of lemon juice for max 5 days. Serve with a dribble of olive oil. 🥰

Shakshuka! Or spicy eggs the way I learned to make it from the Mama/housecleaner at the Moroccan guesthouse I stayed in for a month. As she served it, with an avocado mash, yogurt, and then my toasted homemade sourdough. 🍜

Any time I find beef tongue on special I will always buy it. It's my second favourite part of the head, cheeks as number one.
Parboiled with citrus fruits, garlic, onions, celery, fresh herbs, salt and pepper.
Then sliced thin, dry rubbed, and seared. 🍜

Two more cheeses from Piedmont Italy.
Top: Toma Piedmontese: a buttery cow's milk cheese with a rich salty mushroomy flavor. It's very savory and good.
Bottom: Breise. Kind of a fontina d'aosta clone. I think this one was a little too young and is a little bland to my taste. Comparable to gouda.

There are many egg dishes out there, but all things considered, my favorite might just be Dashimaki, which is a Japanese rolled omelet made with dashi. Having Dashimaki with grated daikon radish for breakfast is truly wonderful!

Today's supper (nothing fancy): a variant on traditional colcannon. In this case, mashed potatoes made with hot milk and butter, chopped bacon, and shredded Baby Gem cabbage fried in bacon fat: with a well in the middle of the colcannon, and more butter melting in it. (Because Ireland.)  :)

Pic from my mom: she has once again made salenjaci, the specialty of Slavonia, the region of Croatia we’re from. They’re like croissant turnovers, made of dough laminated with leaf lard—unrendered, spread pork fat. They don’t taste porky, but they have a unique creaminess.

Rumbledethumps a - traditional Scottish dish of potato, swede & savoy cabbage - cooked, mashed with butter, covered with grated cheese & then baked. Normally accompanies haggis, but not tonight. Supper tonight included spicy beans, pan fried haloumi & bacon. The rumbledethumps were fab 🍜🍽

lao ganma (“old godmother”) is a chinese brand of fried chili crisp condiments (deep fried chili flakes in oil). it is a potent flavor enhancer

white rice + spoonful of lao ganma + fried egg on top is a class chinese college dorm room meal

Paneer butter masala.Goes well with all kinds of flat breads like chapatis and parattas and rotis.

This paneer is called the Indian version of cottage cheese 🧀 made from cow’s milk or buffalo’s milk 

This delicacy was prepared by my daughter 🍜

#indiancuisine

Mia en place for a simple Chicken Cacciatore. That little block in front of the chicken is my secret to making really deep, rich tomato sauces. A hunk of the rind from Parmigiano Reggiano helps offset the often flat flavor of canned tomatoes. 🍽️🍝

Absolutely ruined myself on chanterelle mac and cheese last night. Sautéed mushrooms and shallots in butter and a hint of thyme, mix with elbow mac, a little buttermilk and Point Reyes Toma cheese (from Costco), then topped with Vietnamese fried shallots. Didn't look like much, so no pics... but omg

Important culinary secret for tomato-based sauces: Gochujang. Bolognese? Gochujang. Arrabiata? More gochujang. Chili of any description? You guessed it, gochujang. It hurts to type this because of my finger, but it's worth it to tell you this

青空ごはん部

カツ重 (katsudon) is a hearty and beloved Japanese dish that combines a crispy deep-fried pork cutlet (tonkatsu) with a bowl of fluffy rice, all smothered in a sweet and savory sauce. It's a popular choice for both lunch and dinner, offering a satisfying and flavorful experience.

Tonight's dinner 🍜: Dutch Hachee with braised red cabbage and apple and mashed potatoes. Hachee is a beef and ale stew, but it's flavoured with a slice or two of peperkoek - a sweet Dutch breakfast cake with tons of spices and sugars. So I made that as well.

Introduced my friend, age 63, to his first takoyaki last night, Japanese savory balls of fried dough stuffed with a bit of octopus that is grilled, topped with Worcestershire like sauce, Japanese mayo, and bonito flakes. You're never too old to have new experiences. At Japan Village in Sunset Park.

Ayida Shonibar has written a gorgeous essay about aloo posto, a Bengali dish that defies the famine inflicted by colonizers. The recipe includes transliterated spoken Bengali and a deep dive into shorsher tel, a controversial and incredibly flavorful mustard oil.

Take a steaming bowl of hot rice. Crack a raw egg into it, add a little soy sauce, and mix it up. That's Tamago (Kake) Gohan, the breakfast of champions. To up your TKG game, separate the egg first, whip the whites, mix them in, then mix in the yolk. I once ate this for breakfast for 6m straight.

Food & Wine promised “the secret to the best most classic version” of carbonara and whaddaya know, they also use my “temper pasta water into the eggs” method. I do use bacon most of the time because my regular stores don’t carry guanciale. 🍜🍽️

bouillon is always my go-to but you can always punch up "box" stock by just boiling it ahead of time with vegetable trimmings day of with stuff you already have. If you have the space, saving your trimmings in a freezer bag is the way to go, otherwise, just buy a nice thing of Better Than Bouillon

Made a modified version of Food52's recipe for Spaghetti Napolitan (Japanese Ketchup Spaghetti) — a dish popularized in Japanese cafes in the 50s.

Made from butter, sauteed red pepper, ketchup, sugar, reserved pasta water, and a splash of milk, it tastes like a grown-up version of Spaghetti-O's.

Tonight's dinner 🍜: Xôi mặn (Vietnamese sticky rice). So good! I couldn't find pork floss or chả lụa (Vietnamese ham) when I was out shopping for the ingredients yesterday, so those are home made. This is one of the best things I've ever eaten.

Fresh pasta dough made with rehydrated porcini mushrooms and filled with creamy roasted mushroom paste, finished in a brown butter sauce with garlic, splash of brandy, couple tbsp cream, some water from the porcini, fresh thyme, pecorino.
Doesn't look great, but: FUCK.

Had a go at recreating the burnt onion side you get in some Turkish restaurants last night and fucking hell I am a genius. Grill a wedged red onion until completely burnt on the edges, smother with pomegranate molasses & a splash of red wine vinegar, add loads of sumac & parsley and tuck in.

Maharashtrian veggie delights at Croydon's new Shree Krishna Vada Pav - a comforting misal pav (sprouted bean curry with crunchy sev, coriander and buns); sabudana khichri (tapioca, potato, green chilli & peanuts); and the eponymous vada pav (deep-fried potato patty & chutneys in a bun).

I spent a lot of my life thinking I hated soup - seemed like it was just a bland, watery grave for overcooked vegetables and pasta. But making soup from scratch at home completely changed my opinion! This intensely flavorful pasta e fagioli with fresh crusty rolls is one of my favorite meals. ❤️

Its getting slightly colder right now and the world is burning so I made a massive pan of imam bayildi, and its tomato-ey, garlicky, olive-oil richness is just what I needed to feel a little bit of comfort

Back on my savoury porridge bullshit and can confirm that making the porridge with miso soup paste and stirring through some peanut rayu is probably the best idea I'll have in 2024. This one is topped with kimchi fried tofu and soft boiled egg and is the lunch of champions.

Picked up a shoulder of lamb from Otway Prime and cooked it. It’s the simplest possible thing — an onion, eight garlic cloves, white wine, stock and a tiny splash of pasatta — probably less than a tablespoon. That’s it. Really not a lot of liquid. Five hours at about 140. So good!!

Last year I was roasting squash (halved, cut-side down, as one does) and figured I might as well make use of the cavity, so I tucked a chunk of leek under each squash half and ended up with PERFECT roasted leeks.

Reposting it here in case it helps/inspires any of y'all.

food tangent.

THE simplest Chinese cooking is literally awesomesauce; heat some oil. Add in chopped up ginger and garlic, let it cool down, add salt.

I first had this with Hainan style chicken but the soy sauce chicken from Asian Family Food Center came with a little thing of the stuff! FSCK yeah!

Bucatini all'Amatriciana tonight! Originally from the Sopranos cookbook (laugh if you will, every single recipe I've tried from it has been a BANGER) but tonight I'm using some modifications from Serious Eats. 

Oh, and no guanciale, just pancetta, because I live in the woods. It's perfectly fine.

Available at Japanese convenience stores, this boiled egg is a testament to Japan's exceptional hygiene standards and culinary skill. The yolk is expertly cooked to the brink of setting, infused subtly with salt during the boiling process in delicately salted water. Just yum, no more! 🥚

Yesterday’s twice cooked pork, Japanese style. Miso replaces Doubanjiang, but everything else is the same. Using Mini Leeks over spring onions was a bad choice, since it made the dish more watery that I’d like, especially when combined with the Napa cabbage. Plenty of flavour came through, though. 🍽️

It’s remarkably easy to grow the most expensive spice on Earth in your home garden. 
Saffron grows from bulbs / corms. They bloom in the Fall and produce 3 threads that you want to carefully hand harvest on flowering day. Dry in parchment paper and place in an airtight jar. 🌱

Although they do produce seeds, trembling aspen reproduce almost entirely by cloning. This is a great example of new clones ￼shooting up into a once open meadow from the roots of the trees behind them. 🌿

Born in the tropical forests of Sri Lanka True (Ceylon) Cinnamon is well suited to grow in our zone 9b. This tree can grow up to 50 ft, or be pruned to live well in a container. It will not tolerate temperatures below 40 degrees, be sure to protect it from rare frosts. 🌱

Image via Tanria Elementals

I was in ATX over the weekend visiting some good friends! I went on a chilly morning run at the Lake Creek Trail and came across THESE. 

Maxamilian sunflowers are fall-time bloomers native from NM, USA to SK, CN + MO, OK & TX, USA, and have a wide naturalized distribution eastward. 🌻

🌿

Scrambled eggs! Scrambled eggs! 🍳 This cute native wildflower is often called “scrambled eggs” or “yellow Corydalis.” Across their native range you can find them blooming in and around rocky areas, creek beds, and hillsides from late winter through early fall as temperatures and conditions allow! 🌿

The family of Hawthorn (Crataegus spp) plants is large, but the members all have one thing in common - they’re pure magic! They grow almost anywhere and prolifically produce super healthy flowers and fruits. It’s prime berry season so let's get to know one of the oldest medicinal plants.

Sunflowers may be the symbol of Ukraine, but they're native to North America, where they were first domesticated in the Late Woodland period.

Seeds found in Mexico are all pre-contact, suggesting Spanish priests suppressed them to block their importance in Sun-worship cults.

Once the primary 🌱 agricultural focus of the region, #Meghri #Armenia has has shifted away from Soviet-enforced pomegranate orchards to the more profitable (and perishable) persimmon.

Deprioritized, pomegranates still get packed - but no new orchards get planted. Legacy trees straggle along.

it’s the first wildflower of spring, although it doesn’t look like it! 🌿

Symplocarpus foetidus | skunk cabbage is a wetland plant that can generate their own heat and are the first eastern plant to bloom each year. The stinky little dome flowers are pollinated by flies. 

Araceae, like Monstera

Recently, I have been looking into the Late Pleistocene climate and vegetation of Argentina for a work project, and was delighted to find out that pink peppercorn was - and still is - part of the native flora in the area. Maybe Macrauchenia developed a taste for the spicy tree.

Ilex dedidua | possumhaw 🌿

This little tree was less than a meter tall and growing out of a crack in a mossy granite boulder and was still laden with fruit for migrating birds.

This is one of our rarer hollies in eastern Virginia, and has the odd distinction of being deciduous

Parochetus communis 🌱

This unique, spreading clover produces single gentian-blue flowers, as opposed to the florets containing many tiny flowers found in true clovers (Trifolium).

Beautiful and nitrogen-fixing!

i’ve grown nasturtium in this barrel both the last two summers but this summer i have been rotating the barrel a quarter turn every day when i’m watering (takes a bit of muscle! 💪🏻) and the plant is much fuller and nicer 🌱✨

Potatoes were cultivated by indigenous people across the Americas, but likely 1st domesticated in the Andes region of Peru 7-10,000 years ago from wild species Solanum brevicaule.

Cultivated potatoes are all tetraploid, while wild potatoes can be diploid, tetraploid, or hexaploid.

Had a literal field day with strawberry growers near Charlotte NC!

NC grows the 3rd largest strawberry crop in the US after CA & FL. But unlike those states, most NC strawberries come from small farms.

Today's Gardening Advice: Harry Lauder's Walking Stick is a neat shrub for winter interest. All twisty branches and early catkins. Japanese Beetles and sucker growth can be issues. Like full sun and average garden soil. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Need a flowering tree for a small space? This is Cercis canadensis 'Covey', also sold as 'Lavender Twist.' Flowers form all up and down the stems and the weeping form reaches about 10'. Prefers full sun to partial shade.  They don't tend to do well with dry soil/hot sun. 🌱

The Rainfall tree begins its full blooming session in January.
This one is a bit early, the flowers are beloved by honey bees.
Also called 'quickstick', livestock farmers plant the sticks as poles and have them as living fences, an additional source of protein for their animals.
#flowerreport 🌱

One of our patches of Blue-eyed grass is blooming nicely even though it has been cool and mostly cloudy lately. It looks like grass (except for the blooms) but is in the Iris family. 
2nd photo is a bloom closeup. 🌿 #NativePlants Southern California

I do love Irises. I believe this particular variety is Iris Reticulata Katherine Hodgekin*. #TheSmallThings #SpreadingJoy

*Introduced in 1958 by the rare bulb grower Eliot Hodgekin and named after his wife.

Summer colours (Chilli Peppers) 🌶️
 
Chilli peppers move through a range of colours as they ripen on the vine. As long as the fruit remains solid it is safe to say it is healthy and on its way… 🌱

kitchen-garden.be/diary/summer...

🌱With notes of 🍋 Lemon & 🌹 Rose, fragrant Hippeastrum 'Apple Blossom' (Ludwig & Co., 1954) is a favorite Amaryllis worldwide, can increase into BIG clumps. Left in cool 3-Season Room colors become deeper. Try outdoors Zone 7 at a South wall, Zone 8+ in the open. Amaryllidaceae

The precious Paperwhite Narcissus grows well in Florida, as long as you give it a cooler location to live in. Being one of the few varieties that doesn’t need chill to flower, they will bloom in clusters of showy, fragrant petals. Bulbs can be planted in either soil or a rock bed.

One of the easiest and most fuss-free varieties of cauliflower we have grown! Medium sized heads with kid-friendly mild flavor. Both heat and cold tolerant, though afternoon shade is recommended for hot southern climates. 🌱💚
(link in comments)

With higher temperatures and less rainfall, we are forced to change the way we plant food.

This image shows a row of black eyed peas growing between 10 year old date palm trees. The area was not tilled.

I kinda thing this is how it was done before we got smart. 🌱

One of the 'easy Sunday' plans is to plant some banana and plantain suckers on the farm.
I will plant this one as well, silk banana or some call it golden banana.
It is not the best type of sucker to plant. This is a water sucker, the better ones are sword suckers but it will still grow. 🌱

The case for planting Allium: they are stunning and hardy. They are onion-family and a natural pest deterrent. They naturalize and multiply! They bring lovely height to a garden. Bees and butterflies adore them! 🌱

Romanesco cauliflower.

Wikipedia: "The inflorescence (the bud) is self-similar in character, with the branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal; each bud is composed of a series of smaller buds, all arranged in yet another logarithmic spiral."

Recovering from learning:
1) Pineapples grow from shrubs (not trees)
2) The plant is a perennial and can produce multiple fruits, each taking about a year to grow.
3) Wild pineapples are pollinated mostly by hummingbirds.
4) Of course, there's messed up colonial history of pineapple monoculture.

Thyme is exceptionally easy to grow. It needs well draining soil and full sun, and loves to be trimmed. Waiting until just before flowering ensures the leaves have the most oil available. Our rainy season can damage the plant, but moving it indoors or away from the elements can help. 🌱

Ignore the fact my hand is all sweaty, I was finishing up a run lol…

Happy to report that it’s Texas persimmon (Diospyros texana) season! Persimmon fruits ripen around early August and are a fav among wildlife. TX persimmons have a small range from central and west TX through northeastern Mexico. 🧪

My 15-year-old jade plant recently went through a rough patch where she looked horrible— shriveled, drooping, discolored. Did you know that your plants’ soil can become hydrophobic? Well, a good repotting took care of that, and now my girl is back to her old self. Much to my relief.

Photinia × fraseri 'Little Red Robin'
Aka: red tip photinia and Christmas berry
Of garden origin
A cross between between Photinia glabra and Photinia serratifolia
Reaching just 2 to 3ft in height this lovely evergreen shrub is perfect for winter pot displays and is hardy to -10

🌺 little sweet betsy 🌿

from early april

different species & much more widespread than twisted trillium, which mostly stick to western middle tn & northern al. these are all over the south & follow the mountains up past new york

According to most guidelines, this snapdragon and this salvia, both of which I planted last year and both of which came back this year despite being “annuals”, are bittersweet. Like they’re very pretty but I don’t think we’re in zone 7 anymore where I live 🌱

Considered by some to be the most important plant for pollination in North America, the Goldenrod (Solidago spp) is a gorgeous keystone species. Providing food for hundreds of insects and animals, this fall blooming flower can grow prolifically, even becoming weedy in some areas. 🌱

It’s 34F outside, raining tiny beads of sleet, & my previously barren camellia is in full bloom.I have learned three things about this plant this season: it does not like fall pruning(LOL), it likes plenty of acid fertilizer, and & it thrives in cooler weather .🌱Flower Report  #Gardening #Nature

I love the early spring blooms on this spirea. 🌱 Interestingly enough, I had 3 of these planted: 2 purchased from the nursery and this 1, propagated from a robust one I had at my old house. The other 2 struggled & are definitively dead now. Shows the value of cloning plants that thrive for you.

Next up: the Texas yellowstar, native to Texas; Oklahoma; Arkansas; through to Coahuila. This pretty annual herb is commonly found in prairie-like habitats across north-central and southern east Texas, specifically along the Edwards Plateau. It does best in well-draining sand and limestone. 🌿⭐️

Thought you folks might like to see skunk cabbage. Yes, these are actually flowers. They are one of the earliest spring blooms, so early that they generate heat to melt through snow, if there is any. Which makes a little oasis for winter pollinators

🌱 🧪 🧵 -= Cauliflory =-

1/ Nope, nothing to do with cauliflower - cauliflory is flowering and fruiting from trunks and large woody branches (caulis = stem).

Let's start this thread off with a banger: meet jaboticaba (Plinia cauliflora), the Brazilian "grape"-tree.

TIL: Haas Avocado trees bloom March - May with each flower lasting only 2 days. The flower opens in the morning as a female, and then the following afternoon as a male. Seems wild and while they self pollinate the best harvests come from cross pollinating. 🌱

Books say Lucerne (Medicago sativa subsp. sativa) can be separated from other subspecies by:
Pods spiralling 2, 3 or even 4 times ✅
Flowers pale mauve to violet ✅+🟡!!
From a field margin near #Cambridge #wildflowerhour

Rhododendron loranthiflorum, an epiphytic vireya species from the Solomon Islands. Nocturnally scented white flowers suggest moth pollination.  Some clones are very compact, but my plant is long and lanky. Not sure what’s up with the species name—flowers don’t look much like a Loranthus to me. 🌱

🌱 The Jerusalem artichokes are now taller than my sunflowers from this past summer. It is flowering, and the cold weather should make the tubers taste better. Will not harvest till the stems are done in a few weeks.

Today's Gardening Advice: If you're prepping for spring seed planting, be sure to wash any previously used pots.  Use a cap full of bleach in a gallon of water and allow pots to soak.  Rinse them well.  Bleach bath helps remove mold and potential insect problems.  🌱

Cardinal Basil! A highly fragrant basil that grows to 24” tall. Bright green leaves contrast large, wine red flower clusters that fade to a dusty plum. Beneficial to pollinators, aroma deters common garden pests. 🌱💚

In 1960, David Latimer planted a garden inside of a bottle and sealed it shut. He opened the bottle and watered the plant in 1972 and sealed it for good. It has been a self sustaining ecosystem for 60 years.

Pink evening primrose! Although this spreading perennial has a wide naturalized range, evening primroses are considered native to only Nebraska; Kansas; Missouri; Oklahoma; Texas; though northeastern Mexico where extensive colonies occupy grassy fields. They bloom from late winter through fall. 🌿

Start with a small act of anti capitalism.

Next time you buy a tomato, save the seeds. The slimy stuff on the seed is a sprout inhibiter. Put the seeds onto a tissue or paper kitchen towel. Save it. Dry it. Stash it somewhere dark. 

Next spring, soak in water and place in dirt and grow tomatoes

If you love summer squash, I highly recommend picking up some Lebanese koosa squash seeds.

This fast-growing, high-yield, compact bush plant can produce huge, flavorful fruit that are very versatile in the kitchen.

🍆 squash 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

🌱Due to less rain and higher temperatures, I mow the grass only twice a year (also good for insects and #birds) and I planted mostly mediterranean plants and herbs (olive, thyme, sage, lavender...). They need less water and are beautiful.🍃
Btw: Sage and thyme reproduce by themselves through seeds.

Growing Peppers in the City 🌶️

There are 100s of types of chilli pepper. Capsicum annuum (sweet peppers and chillies) are the most common and extensively cultivated of the five domesticated capsicums… 🌱

kitchen-garden.be/gardening-wh...

A known anti-fungal in the garden, Marigold assists with human ailments, too! It’s very good for skin issues and discolorations. It promotes the growth of healthy cell tissue of all types. Digestive and menstrual issues can be assisted with Marigold teas or culinary infusions!

As long as there's good drainage, Borage grows in most soil types even in containers! Known to be a great companion plant in the garden as a decoy and is said to improve the flavor of plants it's grown with. Pruning flowers will encourage more blooming plus they’re delicious!

📷:Tanria Elementals

my greatest plant discovery to date, a county record for Iris verna | dwarf iris 🌿

a truly tiny plant with full iris flowers that loves sandy places. I found this, the bigger of two populations in Wise County, VA, in an old forest fire site that brimmed with botanical wonders

Proud of this Baptisia australis (false blue indigo) plant for exceeding 1 foot in height. I started my Baptisia plants from seed last year, and... yeah, it's going to be a while before they get to full size. Which is fine. I love growing from seed, even though native perennials are slow. 🌱

Multiplier onions are a win! Bought these after learning about them from SkillCult. Did not disappoint. Started from sets 2 months ago. Harvested sustainably for gronions as they grew, and each single set tripled or quadrupled.

Anyone else growing multipliers / potato onions?
🌱

🌱 In #syracuse #ny this forsythia bush is blooming in my neighbor’s yard. It should bloom in the spring, so this is wicked early.   Flowers blooming out of season is a bad omen. For example, spring flowers blooming in the winter means the temperatures are too warm & that has lots of ramifications.

This snazzy little flower is a rain lily, Zephyranthes atamasca is native to a chunk of the Southeast but threatened in some of its range. I planted them lining the walkway in the back. They look like a clump of chives most of the year, but after a good rain, they flower (hence the name.)

Sowed my first Spring seeds at the allotment 😀🌱

This time each year, I put all my leftover salad stuff leaves in a packet, and sprinkle some every couple of weeks. Gives me signals on how the soil is warming, when and if they germinate. And I usually get some early salads, too. 🌱👩‍🌾

The Texas mountain laurels are officially in full bloom! Not a true mountain laurel, it’s a small shrub-like evergreen in the pea family endemic to central and west Texas through northern Mexico. Their blooming season (March through April) fills the air with a STRONG smell resembling grape soda! 🌿

Oh I forgot one of my most esoteric accomplishments of the day: I found out at least two of my pawpaw seeds, after over a year of coaxing along, are sprouting. One from each batch, too, so I can get different genetics

This variety is a mutation of the species with no pigments in the flower other than yellow. I wish I also have the natural form, which has spots on the dorsal petal and more colors.

🌱 Paphiopedilum insigne var. sanderianum

#orchids #plants #gardening

Tomato seedlings for high tunnel production. This is Kakao and Caiman.
I do not recommend these varieties for home gardens bc they both require protection to produce to their potential.
🌱
Kakao is similar to Kumato but a little better flavor and higher productivity in the hoop house.

A little Sunday trivia for ya:

I love Trillium 🤍 seeing them means spring is coming. It’s also Wisconsin’s State Flower. Don’t pick them because doing so can disrupt the plant’s ability to sustain itself and it can take years to bloom again- once established they can live 100 years!

Singleaf piñon, Pinus monophylla, is the only single-needle pine - all others have 2-5 needles in bundles. It has large seeds, piñones, that are delicious. Piñones are important to Native Americans throughout the  range of the tree. This is an old tree on the side of a canyon near Petrified Forest.

Today's Gardening Advice: Magnolia virginiana, or Sweetbay Magnolia, is an excellent native small tree/shrub.  It produces creamy white, heavily scented flowers throughout summer.  May retain some foliage in winter.  Tolerates moist soil.  Tends to be multi-stemmed. 🌱

Shire Giant Sunflower, for all of you delightful sunflower fans!
These easy to grow giants reach up to 12' tall. The heads grow up to 14" across & weigh 6 lbs! A favorite of bees & birds alike, they also make excellent living poles for climbing beans. 🌱💚🌻
Sunflowers symbolize loyalty & adoration.

Some wildflowers need a period of freezing temps in order to sprout in spring. We plant ours in fall and let nature take care of the rest. Our Rocket Larkspur is a little confused by the warm weather! 🌱💚 #gardening

🌱 Perennial hollyhocks (Alcalthaea suffrutescens "Parkallee & "Parkfrieden") in the morning light of the autumn equinox. They bloom from July until October (sometimes longer) and are very popular with wild bees.

My heath aster is blooming! I’m so excited about this stuff. I planted 3-4 babies last fall and it has spread beautifully. Keeps down weeds, flowers in fall when the pollinators need food, and is native to much of N America. Seems hardy af so far. Why don’t I see this everywhere as ground cover? 🌱

This is the perfect season to grow Lemon Balm in Florida! Your plant will be flourishing with leaves, which should be harvested often. As the intense heat of Summer arrives, the plant will wilt and possibly die back. Cut it back and in the Fall it will likely come back to life! 

📷 Tanria Elementals

Today's garden job was transplanting lupins from my nursery bed into the borders. These are so easy to grow from seed as a short-lived perennial and they self-seed everywhere on my #allotment. Much more resistant to slugs than plants from the garden centre! 😊🌱

Winter planting 🌱

Garlic is one of the easiest crops to grow. The cloves of a bulb are split and planted with the point upwards. These can be started indoors in pots or directly planted outside… 🌱

kitchen-garden.be/diary/winter...

just learned that cotton is a mallow (i've never seen it in flower, just yoinked standing dry stalks off the roadside in oklahoma) and now understand why the weird prickly seed heads of hollyhocks are vaguely familiar... plants are so cool dude

Unbelievable these tomatoes 🍅 were still growing in the veg patch a week ago, however, as green as apples. After a week in a paper bag in the cupboard under the stairs, they are now perfectly ripe and ready to enjoy.

Wintersowing. Yesterday I moved these from a place of too much sun, where in this current weather they might germinate too soon, to a spot I hope is cooler. Way too early for them to sprout! (Kale, collards, mustard, flax, sage, coreopsis, poppies....)

Today's Gardening Advice: Certain wildflowers deserve to stay right in the woods.  Various orchids and trillium are difficult to establish in the average garden.  Orchids like the common lady slipper have symbiotic relationships with fungus.  Gardeners are too often tempted to try them and fail. 🌱

Many daisies in Aotearoa (New Zealand) are shrubs or small trees. Heketara (Olearia rani) is flowering now. This is a cluster of tiny florets: 12 outer white ray florets each have a bilobed stigma; 5 inner yellow disk florets, each with 5 stamens joined in a tube & a stigma that pushes out pollen.

The Tamarisk trees are also in full flower right now. Quite a special tree, being drought and salt tolerant. I know it is invasive in some countries but at home here in Spain. 
I only noticed the heavily pollen laden bee in the background afterwards. 🐝🌱🦉

Nasturtiums are a must for any garden. Beautiful, unusual, and fully edible. The bees also go nuts for them.

Not sold? They also love poor soil and will help fix it, great companion plants to deter pests, and perfect cut flowers.

🌺 nasturtiums 🌸 flowers 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

For fellow gardeners surrounded/ shaded out by black walnut trees 🌱: I sowed 3 tomato plants (sun gold) in my backyard, mere feet from the canopy edges of the dreaded juglone zone. Absolutely unaffected. Further context: my death-strip is the south canopy edges where squirrels drop the most husks.

Note to self...do more research before planting a tree. Case in point: Wanted lovely fast-growing tree to shade house. Disappointed when realized Kentucky coffee tree is leafless almost 9 months of yr & extremely messy once it starts dropping huge leaves (60-90 cm & largest of any Canadian tree). 🌱

sowing Canna seed today ..all the years  have been gardening and teaching horticulture i had never grown them form seed until last year , but of a faff as you have to Nic the seed , which have a very very hard coat and its easy to cut your finger off then they have to be soaked in water for a week

🌸 downy lobelia 🌿

little late-season lobelia with just one flower left, great opportunity to get a good look at its structure. through the split separating the two small upper lobes you can see the corolla tube that supports the reproductive parts of the flower

A few years ago I bought some pawpaws (Asimina triloba) because I wanted to try growing them in the inland Northwest. Two of the four grafted trees have survived, but they are STRUGGLING. The rootstock survived on one of the dead ones and it is growing so much better than the selected stock. 😡🌱

Are those green flowers? Yep. These are Edwards Plateau five-eyes. They are summer bloomers but if you couldn’t tell from my other posts, we’re headed in that direction fast & early this year. A Chamaesaracha sp., this flower is native to central and south central Texas, preferring rocky habitats. 🌿

our three year old dátil pepper is putting out adorable tiny fruit this early in the season - this is a very, very hot (but not ultra-hot) fruity variety from St. Augustine, Florida. Delicious, I love these guys.

In Sydney for the next few weeks, and it’s noticeable how much more common brush turkeys are here in the suburbs than 10 or 20 years ago. The jacarandas are a sight (in October, thanks to the very warm spring; traditionally they don’t bloom until November.)

Hydrangeas, I love them. This particular plant makes me laugh. I liberally dosed it with blue colour maker aluminium sulfate but the alkaline game is strong in my soil so I have a multi- coloured bush this year. Can’t say I mind. 🤣😂🤣

🇳🇿
🌱🇳🇿

Sunchokes (Helianthus tuberosus) and sunflowers (H. annuum) can hybridize. The difficulty is that sunchokes bloom so late.

Fortunately, the giant sunflowers had a few secondary flowers that were shedding pollen. Now I just need to watch for seeds.

This is another of my favorite plants. It flowers in mid summer on leafless stalks with up to 10 flowers that open successively. They are sometimes called pink naked ladies. This one is a rarer hot pink variety. 🌱

Amaryllis belladonna

First white dandelion of the spring.  We grow dandelions for chicken treats and occasional salad greens, but it gets boring growing the same old yellow ones.  Taraxacum albidum for the botany geeks. 🌱

This week: Ericaceae are a cosmopolitan family with diverse habits. The morphological innovation that unites them all is the presence of appendages on the anthers, seen here on this dissected flower of Vaccinium pallidum. #Ericaceae #Botany #PlantScience 🌾🧪

Menyanthes trifoliata L., Bogbean, flowers in late May. I know of 2 populations, one on the village pond, planted, & a wild pop. in the local fen/bog/mire (right). A monotypic genus, it has 2 morphs, like Primula, pin-eyed & thrum-eyed. The cultivated ones are pin-eyed, & the wild ones, thrum-eyed.

Two of our favourite allotment crops, Florence fennel and celeriac. The fennel was sown early to get a good start to harvest bulbs in June before it bolts, celeriac was sown early because it takes until October for the tiny seedlings to finally produce 1-2 kg roots.
#Allotment 🌱

In 2004, mathematicians at King's College London conducted a study commissioned by Sky Movies which concluded that THE SHINING was the 'perfect scary movie' due to a proper balance of elements including shock value, suspense, escalating music and isolated setting.

This was pointed out in an AV Club write-up for the greatest horror movies of the 80s, but it bears repeating, as it sure feels right: The Thing is the greatest special effects movie ever made.

The way I see it, there isn't a bit of CGI work out there that can't compare w/ the practical FX here.

📽️

I've never heard anyone say MAD MAX (1979) is the best MAD MAX film, and it's not, but it warrants re-evaluation. It's scarily relevant.

MAD MAX isn't post-apocalyptic. It's about how the apocalypse sets in, as society gradually dies around us. It's about giving up on the fantasy of civilization. 📽️

Shocked THE BOYS NEXT DOOR isn't better known. Spheeris' film is certainly worthy of more attention/acclaim.  Among other things, it's a great example of the "female gaze." Seldom do you see the camera so entranced with the beauty of a movie's male stars.  It's also a film of rare tenderness/empathy

Angel Heart [1987] dir: Alan Parker
A slow burn with an obvious spoiler right at the start but still an enjoyable 'thriller'. Parker excels here with the Trevor Jones score and Courtney Pine's saxophone adding texture to the lush cinematography. Still consider this Mickey Rourke's best performance.

Delighting in one of my true comfort films, Pacific Rim, put through a monochrome filter

Stripped of colour you're left with only contrasts, with details, and you see the film as something different. Harsher and raw in a beautiful way
More Metropolis than monster thriller

enraptured by the lurid pink/green two-tone technicolor palette of Doctor X (1932).

directed by Michael Curtiz (Casablanca), this pre-code mad scientist horror movie was so clearly one of the many inspirations behind the Rocky Horror Picture Show.

Watched Hellboy (2004) tonight and surprised at how well it holds up, in it's clear storytelling & effects. It's nearly 20 years old, but Del Toro's choices with the effects were really good ones, hardly any uncanny shocks. Can't say that for some big budget movies half that age. Recommend.

“Jane Greer, Ida Lupino, Robert Ryan dine together at San Francisco's Embarcadero restaurant before the world premiere of ‘Hard, Fast And Beautiful.’” — Screenland, Oct. 1951

Lupino produced and directed this movie. Ryan had a cameo with Lupino in it.

I watched Michael Caine thriller The Black Windmill (Don Siegel, 1974).

It'd just be an ok, slow-burn spy/actioner, but stylish direction, a great cast filled out w top character actors (see alt text), & excellent location work, incl a hovercraft crossing of the Channel, make it quite a lot of fun.

For international women's day, here are four exploitation films directed by women. 📽️

Terminal Island - Stephanie Rothman

Stripped to Kill - Katt Shea

The Slumber Party Massacre - Amy Holden Jones

Kissed - Lynne Stopkewich

WHEN EVIL LURKS is the best horror movie I've seen this year. Like a twisted and brutal occult detective story, it lays out the rules for the characters and punishes those who deviate from protocol. Heavy-duty trigger warnings abound, so please check the label before proceeding.

This weekend's watch. SEX, LIES & VIDEOTAPE (1989).  Haven't seen it in 25 years at least. Forgot how good it is.

Made for a scant $1M, it won the grand prize at Cannes, & Spader won best actor. Brilliantly written. Marvelous characters.

If you haven't watched it in awhile, highest recommendation.

the amazing mr x is a twisty, highly-atmospheric horror/thriller from eagle-lion films about a phony spiritualist. it’s pretty great… pretty low budget but the photography is beautiful and the script is tight and clever. shades of the uninvited and nightmare alley….this one is legit. check it out. 📽️

Bringing Out The Dead doesn't get its due, both as a Scorsese film and as a film capturing the spirit of the late 90s, with possibly Nicholas Cage's best performance. 

It's surprisingly gothic in atmosphere and manages to capture Scorsese's twin passions of New York grime and human spirituality.

Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat. One of the most platonically perfect movies I’ve ever seen. The dancing is at the  ‘impossible’ level of difficulty. One is reminded of watching Bruce Lee. That these two should also be great at screwball comedy boggles the mind.

Thief (1981) is so much better than Heat (1995), not only for omitting the disgusting and unnecessary Waingro sequences—scenes whose only function is "proving the bad guys are bad" and inevitably the proof is violence against working women, but I think it has a different view of commitment

Shock Treatment (1981) might not be as iconic as it's predecessor Rocky Horror but this almost forgotten sequel sure beats it in set and costume design.
Worth a watch just for the homophobia song that rhymes faggots with maggots.

Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about a door-to-door salesman who is lured into crime by a teenage prostitute.  Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.  

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, who was BOTD in 1936

The City of the Dead AKA Horror Hotel (1960)

One of the great Gothic horror movies

Directed by John Llewellyn Moxey

Starring Christopher Lee, Vanetia Stevenson, and Betta St. John, who was BOTD in 1929

10 #horror films I rate highly, in honour of 🎃 month:

1. Berberian Sound Studio
2. Martin
3. Vampyr
4. Henry Portrait Of A Serial Killer
5. Dark Water (2002)
6. The Witch
7. A Tale Of Two Sisters
8. The Wailing 
9. yellowbrickroad
10. The Asphyx

(Not my top 10, but overlaps with it)

Miller’s Girl (2024) Instant Classic. Simmering. 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘭𝘺 tension; but a classical plot line many are failing to grasp. Layering woven thru spoken word, maturation of longing & framing like Minnelli. Literature was vehicle for stealth Gothic Romance. If u don’t like books, you’ll hate this #filmsky

This stylish first film by the Cohen Brothers is clearly done as inexpensively as possible, but the plot is as rich and as fully formed as one might expect from them. There’s some fantastic writing, acting, and plot twists throughout. 

…/ 1 of 2

Just saw Talk to Her (2002)… I’ve been gradually working my way through Almodóvar’s filmography, and this is surely the best one. Very strange, very uncomfortable. I admire his ability to grapple with the most problematic concepts.

18/31

INVASION OF THE BODY SNATCHERS (1978) is not only the best film adaptation of the story, it's also one of the most frightening sci-fi movies ever made. A quiet apocalypse, where the end is written on expressionless faces, background shadows, and whispered conspiracies. A horror masterpiece.

Just rewatched Week-End in Havana (1941), which is getting to be one of my comfort movies. It’s one of my favorite Fox musicals… the songs aren’t too good (except for the 3 Carmen Miranda numbers), but it’s a wonderful screwball comedy. All the characters are great and the Technicolor is magnificent

It's funny after all "it's what you DON'T SEE that's scarier!" types pearl clutching over Carpenter's THE THING to rewatch that movie and realize what a generally low key and restrained move it is...until it needs to let things off the chain.

Last night, I rewatched Lake Mungo for the first time since I saw it maybe 15 years ago. And let me tell you, this movie is effective! I had full-body goosebumps multiple times. No jump scares, just eerie as fuck. Great storytelling, too. I wish more people made them like this

Dreamchild (1985)

A sadly neglected British fantasy film in which now elderly Alice Hargreaves, the inspiration for Lewis Carroll's Alice books, reflects on her childhood relationship with Carroll.

Screenplay by Dennis Potter

Directed by Gavin Millar, who was BOTD in 1938

Watched The Doorway to Hell (1930) this afternoon… an early Warner Bros gangster film, and James Cagney’s 2nd film appearance. Not especially notable, except for how great Mr Cagney is. He’s every inch the gangster tough guy here, it’s obvious why they quickly started giving him lead roles.

I watched Terminator 2 last night for the first time since 1991. Action scenes still amazing, Robert Patrick still terrifying, Linda Hamilton still badass, all the stuff with the kid still incredibly stupid & poorly acted, final thumb up still the most cringe-inducing thing I’ve ever seen. A++/D-

The Thing. 1982. dir: John Carpenter

There’s a LOT to like about this film: the ensemble cast, Carpenter’s direction, Ennio Morricone’s score, Rob Bottin’s astounding physical effects work, the tense paranoia among the crew, and Dean Cundey’s exceptional cinematography.

Remembering Jean-Luc Godard, BOTD in 1930.  Here are my picks for his ten best films:

1. Pierrot le fou
2. Numero Deux
3. La Chinoise
4. Band of Outsiders
5. Vivre sa vie
6. JLG/JLG
7. Goodbye to Language
8. Weekend
9. Germany Year 90 Nine Zero
10. The Image Book

The Pumpkin Eater (1964)

Classic British drama about a woman who seems to get pregnant every time the wind blows and her unhappy marriage to a screenwriter.  

Starring Anne Bancroft

Screenplay by Harold Pinter

Directed by Jack Clayton, BOTD in 1921

Shocktober ‘23 #16: EVIL DEAD 2

A perfect movie. Campbell is so hot and such a movie star. Raimi is in his element mixing Looney Toons, Three Stooges, and EC horror comics with the exuberance of a man who knows he is getting one over on the studio who is paying. Everything I love about movies.

Something Wicked This Way Comes is the best of the dark Disney era, by far. The perfect balancing act between nostalgia for a childhood that is almost over and nostalgia for a childhood that is long past is bittersweet and beautiful. 1/2

just watched TALK TO ME, a pretty boilerplate possession story punched up w excellent performances, sturdy emotional core & a well executed, properly depressing addiction allegory. the hand is silly but it doesnt matter, it opens the door to something much bigger & they pull it off w flying colors

The Haunting at 60: is it still one of the scariest films ever made?: Martin Scorsese and Steven Spielberg count the slow-burn 1963 horror as one of the greatest of all time but it wasn’t always seen as a classic

Went to the World's Fair last night. Good stuff. Can see why it's tough to market and describe since it's not really a horror film but a meditation on conjuring the real out of artifice, and a portrait of Gibson's eversion. Cyberspace permeating even the nowhere-places.

Finished watching Rollerball. Brilliant. 

I especially like the fragments of subplot intentionally left only partly formed. It's there to put it together but it doesn't shove it down your throat.

Obviously a huge influence on Blood Bowl but also perhaps on 2000AD's Song of the Surfer

Just watched The Long Good Friday for the first time. Pretty wild British gangster film, with a beautiful, perfect ending. Also young Pierce Brosnan pointing the gun at the end looks like Cousin Gregg from Succession.

Alfre Woodard - 📽️ Crooklyn (Spike Lee, 1994)

Woodard is one of our finest actors, but rarely showcased as well as her ace mothering in Crooklyn. Her romantic dynamic with Delroy Lindo has lived history, and her monologues (as in Love & Basketball, 12 Years a Slave, Clemency…) are inimitably divine.

TAXI! reminded me a lot of gritty 70s movies like THE LAST DETAIL.  Like them, it's a downbeat meditation on male anger and alienation.  Cagney's superb as a cab driver with a hair trigger temper.  As for Young, she does the best she can with the underwritten role of Cagney's love interest.

I WALK ALONE is worth seeing for its blockbuster cast alone, but the gorgeous photography and high growing tension ain’t too shabby. It’s one of those icy, glossy, detached noir films that I don’t fall for quite as much as the grittier stuff; it’s still essential (albeit uneven) #Noirvember viewing.

Julianne Moore - Cast a Deadly Spell (Martin Campbell, 1991)

This “fantasy detective” HBO film is unlike much you’ve seen, taking place in a Los Angeles full of zombies, gargoyles, and magic. Moore is a great tone performer and goes exactly large enough as the (possible) femme fatale lounge singer.

A film that’s haunted me since it’s release in 1990 is Jacob's Ladder. Vietnam vet Tim Robbins sees demons wherever he goes & has no idea why. The film's all practical special effects have had an outsized impact on pop culture, influencing everything from other movies to video games, eg Silent Hill

watch Ravenous (1999) for a film that combines a sharp critique of manifest destiny, a suggestion that cannibalism somehow gives you super powers, deep homoeroticism and one of the wildest tones you've ever seen - all in one really weird movie

If you're looking for an awesome 'so bad it's incredible' movie I highly recommend Maximum Overdrive. It's Stephen King's first (and only) directorial debut fueled entirely by cocainium. 
I made a video essay on the story behind the scenes that lead to this disaster

youtu.be/gfJ_9r8jDho?...

Metropolis / Fritz Lang, dir. (1927)
Man with a Movie Camera / Dziga Vertov, dir. (1929)
Un Chien Andalou / Luis Buñuel, dir. (1929)
I Was Born, But… / Yasujirō Ozu, dir. (1932)
 
“…somewhere between chance and mystery lies imagination, the only thing that protects our freedom…” Luis Buñuel
📽️

Protea (1913) played by Josette Andriot. (Probably) the first femme fatale / super-criminal in cinema. A master of disguise doing heists in a black acrobat suit, two whole years before Feuillade's Les Vampires.

Just watched The Exorcist for the first time in ages. What a great movie. Makes me think about how important it is for the world to feel lived-in in a horror movie, and to be populated by characters who feel like they live in it/have lives outside the events of the movie. Makes the stakes feel real.

The Brotherhood of Satan (1971)

A wonderfully strange horror movie about a small town controlled by a coven of elderly Satan worshippers.  Early 70s cult horror at its finest.  

Directed by Bernard McEveety

Cinematography by John Arthur Morrill, BOTD in 1935

I wouldn't call THE MIND READER a great movie, but it is a fun and fascinating one.  Warren William is excellent as a fake fortune teller with no conscience or desire to reform.  And the film's quite visually distinctive, with the action often shot at weird, disorienting angles.

Images from films starring Kaycee Moore, BOTD in 1944.  She only acted in five movies, but three of them are absolute classics.

Killer of Sheep (1978)
Daughters of the Dust (1991)
Bless Their Little Hearts (1984)

Kelly Lynch - 📽️ Drugstore Cowboy (Gus Van Sant, 1989)

Lynch radiates so much cool in this film that it’s hard to dislike her no matter where the story goes. Her relationship with a poetic, superstitious Matt Dillon becomes a pained, raw, real dynamic of struggles between addiction and sobriety.

My god, Cronenberg's THE FLY is so profoundly sad. It could've been a cheap shocker, but Jeff Goldblum portrays a character that we *genuinely* care for. If you've ever had a loved one with a degenerative disease, this hits like a sledgehammer.

Ed Lachman’s cinematography for EL CONDE is mesmerizing: its exquisite, uncanny abstractions of shadow and light are the film’s main artistic triumph, at times genuinely evocative of Dreyer and Murnau in its visual and spiritual depth. Sumptuous, awe-striking work.

A recent online poll judged Monty Python's Holy Grail to be the best film of the 70s. Considering that decade gave us the first two Godfathers, Jaws, Star Wars, Dog Day Afternoon, and Chinatown (to name but a few), I'm not persuaded we have 20/20 hindsight yet. Even Rocky Horror holds up better.

Spider Baby, or the Maddest Story Ever Told (1967)

A truly out there horror comedy about a trio of insane, child-like siblings.  Would make a great double bill with Eraserhead.  

Directed by Jack Hill, BOTD in 1933

Just revisited Miami Vice (2006). It has the same problem I have with most of Mann’s movies (heavy on style, light on substance). But it’s an exceedingly stylish movie, Mann has a hell of an eye. I just wish it wasn’t entirely bereft of thought. But it’s fun and it moves and it looks terrific.

Daisy Doodad's Dial (1914)

A silent comedy short about a face-pulling contest.  Florence Turner, who directed and stars, puts on a tour de force exhibition of facial contorting.  Turner was BOTD in 1885.  She's historically noteworthy as one of the first movie stars.

DAVID HOLMES: THE BOY WHO LIVED (2023, dir: Hartley) a genuinely moving portrait of Daniel Radcliffe’s stunt double, who was paralyzed by an on-set accident gets perverted by the filmmakers insistance on making the story uplifting and redemptive despite what the interviewees actually say.

I watched Lake Mungo last night. 

I read on wikipedia the writer/director said - "I don't think it's a supernatural thriller. I think it's meant to be an exploration of grief."
I don't think it's a supernatural thriller but it's one of the most realistic "hauntings" I've seen even if unintentional-

I've posted a ton of comedy, so to keep with the somber  mood set by The Wailing, let's talk about Saint Maud. What an amazing movie, with an all out performance by Morfydd Clark. Twin horrors of of religious zeal and being medically reliant on a stranger co-mingle here wonderfully, horrifcally.

Diana Rigg passed away three years ago today. My favorite film of hers is THE ASSASSINATION BUREAU (1969, Basil Dearden). And it's comforting that her final film was also one of her best: LAST NIGHT IN SOHO (2021, Edgar Wright). Her timing and line delivery remained priceless.

Rewatched All The President’s Men (1976) tonight, it’s so great. Every journalism film in the last 40 years has been chasing this one. I think the key is a laser-tight focus on the investigation. No bullshit love story, no subplots, no whatever. It never stops moving, and it’s flooded with details.

#Watched The Boys Next Door (1985) - goddamn! Think ‘80s spring break teen comedies full of shenanigans, except Spheeris moves the sociopathic nature of the leads from unexamined subtext to explicit text. Just watched Cry of the City (1948), a terrific noir film with Richard Conte as an escaped murderer trying to escape the city. Fast paced, hard dialogue, and exceedingly good performances by Conte and Victor Mature. Highly recommended for noir aficionados.

30 mins left, so here’s today’s “thing I love” - Lake Mungo. A perfect example of low budget ingenuity, and one of the most innovative horror films of its decade, maybe the 21st century so far, in storytelling terms. Heart breaking, profoundly creepy, a testament to the power of horror storytelling

The Draughtman's Contract (1982)

Yuen Qiu - 📽️ Kung Fu Hustle (Stephen Chow, 2004)

Need I elaborate? The Landlady of Pigsty Alley is the greatest image and character of any Chow movie, a strong-willed snarker who steals the movie and fires sonic screams that destroy all matter. It’s one of the peak comedy performances.

🎥 filmsky

Quentin Dupieux’s Smoking Causes Coughing gave me some of my hardest laughs of the year. Deeply silly, kinda lovely, best environmental message movie this year. Power Rangers via Tales From the Crypt. Great stuff.

Man it’s so amazing and special to see a movie and instantly understand why it’s called one of the best movies of all time. Jacques Tati’s “Playtime” was so hilarious and chaotic, singularly artistically ambitious and brilliant. I keep laughing just thinking about it  youtu.be/zrYB8hgyq4s?...

Meshes of the Afternoon (1943)

There sure are a lot of shots of Maya Deren's feet in this landmark experimental film.  Was Quentin Tarantino involved somehow?  

Directed by Deren and her husband, Alexander Hammid.  Hammid was BOTD in 1907.

Posters for REPEAT PERFORMANCE (1947), a fascinating, Twilight Zone-ish noir.  

The movie's currently available on the Criterion Channel if you're looking for something cool to watch this holiday weekend.

THOROUGHBREDS, starring Anya Taylor-Joy and Olivia Cooke, is now streaming on Hulu. A ruthlessly stylish, razor-sharp, pitch-black psychodrama, set in a picture-perfect enclave where two privileged, amoral teens hatch a murder plot and practice the technique of crying on command.

Tonight I saw St Drogo. It’s low-budget, queer, slow-burn, folk horror. I loved the cinematography, and the interesting critical takes on status & community. I also think there’s a lot going on about the nature of desire, and the diverse ways it’s regulated by religion, money, and social groups.

1/So The Incredibles is one of my favorite movies, but I hadn't really thought of the moment at the beginning - where Mr. Incredible talks about the world never staying "saved" and how he feels like the maid - as particularly profound. In a movie, saving the world is done once and for good.

MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. Love the absurd logic of Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins blackmailing Victor Mature and compelling him to take her to his country estate so that she can learn to be a lady.  Love even more that we are supposed to root for her and want her scheme to succeed.

The Dunwich Horror (1970) is a very horny yet deeply unsexy movie. Silly and hamfisted—but I did like the psychedelic representation of the monster. “Mind-altering” works just as well to describe encounters with unfathomable being as it does the effects of hallucinogenics

Thinking about Resolution (2012), a charming and DIY two-hander cosmic horror film that does so much with so little, anchored by two wonderfully real performances. Hang-out energy but incredibly eerie.

For Apollo 15, 16, and 17, NASA sent up the battery powered Lunar Roving Vehicle so the astronauts could extend their range as they explored the moon. Weighing only 76 lbs on the moon, the moon buggy helped the astronauts transport equipment and samples around, and all three are still on the moon!

Space telescope spies neutron star in the debris of famous supernova

In 1987, a star in a nearby galaxy went supernova and its fiery demise was detected in Earth’s nighttime sky with the naked eye for months.

Using a pulsar timing array, we look for correlated noise which might be consistent with the signal of a gravitational wave passing by Earth stretching and compressing space (on the scale of the GALAXY!) in a predictable way. 🧪🔭👩🏻‍🔬📡

We found the most distant (to-date) active black hole at the center of a galaxy ~570 Myrs after the Big Bang (>13.2B yrs ago)!! 🧪

🧪 Astronomers have discovered the largest and oldest mass of water ever detected in the universe. 

Circling the supermassive black hole of a quasar 12 billion light years away is a cloud of water vapour with 140 trillion times more water than is found in Earth’s oceans.

It intrigues all: what happens to the stuff that falls into a black hole? Physicists hate singularities that destroy all information so Einstein&Rosen proposed the wormhole, like the plug hole you’re imagining with an exit.  Hawking radiation also returns the info to the Universe but v slowly 🔭🧪

Though all these galaxies appear near each other, most are separated by hundreds of millions of light-years, except two. Galaxy IC 1947 on the left side is the closest to NGC 1356, the large galaxy on the right. They are about 400,000 light-years apart. Credit: NASA/ESA. 🔭 🧪

A white dwarf star that ate its worlds bears the scars of cannibalism, as a metal imprint in its surface shaped by the star's magnetic field.

X marks the spot! These X-shaped diffraction spikes highlight an active supermassive black hole in galaxy Markarian 817. 

It is driving material out of the galaxy, with some of the outflow raining back onto the galaxy, and the rest settling into space. 🔭 🧪

Credit: NASA, ESA, STScI.

The most energetic single particle ever detected, a cosmic ray dubbed the "Oh-My-God" particle, was observed by the Fly's Eye Cosmic Ray Detector OTD in 1991.

Its energy was about 3.2 x 10²⁰ eV ~ 51 J, equivalent to a baseball moving at almost 60 mph. 🧪 🔭

Image: Olena Shmahalo/Quanta Magazine

Holy crap, the Euclid commissioning images look absolutely incredible. I can't wait to see what it tells us about dark matter, dark energy, and how galaxies form & evolve. (Euclid will observe galaxies over 1/3 of the full sky, as opposed to JWST, which sees deeper over a much smaller area.)  🧪🔭

The JWST found the most distant supermassive black hole (SMBH) yet! It's so far that it existed when the Universe was only 570 million years old. This SMBH is a whopping 9 million times the mass of the sun. It was found in the CEERS survey in a galaxy called CEERS 1019. 1/

The narrow jets that formed HH 1 and HH 2 are more visible in different wavelengths. The jet in HH 1, at the top right, is evident in visible light, whereas the jet toward HH 2, at the bottom left, is clearer in infrared light. 🧪 🔭

In distant space outside our galaxy is... something big. Known as the Great Attractor, it's the gravitational centre of the Laniakea Supercluster, millions of times as massive as the Milky Way, and we can't see it. The galaxy itself is in the way! But gravitational waves aren't blocked by matter.

Happy Galaxies Day! A century ago tonight, Edwin Hubble exposed a photograph at the 100-inch telescope on Mt. Wilson whose interpretation enlarged our mental Universe incomparably. Hubble recognized that a tiny brightening speck in what was then called the Andromeda Nebula had to be a Cepheid...

The intricate Simeis 147, or "Spaghetti Nebula," reveals twisting filaments covering nearly 3 degrees in the Taurus-Auriga boundary. Composed of hydrogen (red) and oxygen (blue) emissions, it spans 150 light-years at an estimated distance of 3,000 light-years.

Mars is a stunning fossil. The planet isn’t geographically active like Earth. 

A new study reports the discovery of cracks in the Martian mud that are billions of years old. This means ancient Mars had wet-dry cycles, which suggests the possibility of life. 1/2

A little over a week after successfully landing a rover on the moon, India on Saturday launched its first solar mission aimed at studying the outer layers of the sun. Aditya L1 weighs about 3,300 pounds and will travel a distance of about 930,000 miles over four months. nyti.ms/3R5a8J6

In orbit around the Milky Way are dozens of satellite galaxies, each containing millions or even billions of stars. In their wakes, tidal forces draw out streamers of gas and stars, some of which are ancient. Just imagine the view any planets in these streams might have in their night skies.

The Mercury-Atlas 6 mission launched OTD in 1962. John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth, circling three times in the Friendship 7 capsule. 

Glenn refused to trust the computer controlled trajectory until mathematician Katherine Johnson had checked it by hand. 👩🏾‍🔬 🔭

The delicate rings of Saturn. Remember, the rings aren’t solid objects but are instead composed of billions of bits of ice. In some places, you can see right through the rings!

(16) Psyche was discovered by Annibale de Gasparis at Napoli in 1852; the 22-petatonne asteroid is about 220 km across and its heliocentric orbit is 2.53 x 3.32 AU x 3.1 deg. NASA's Psyche probe is scheduled to reach Psyche in 2029.

No mirror image here! These are two interacting galaxies, known as NGC 5331. This pair will collide and merge over hundreds of millions of years. They are 450 million light-years away from Earth in the constellation Virgo.

The sixth largest moon of Saturn, Enceladus, is arguably the most enthralling. Ice volcanoes explode from its south pole, creating a captivating sight. These cryovolcanoes cover the landscape with fresh snow, making Enceladus the most reflective surface in the solar system.

When our Sun dies (a long time from now) it will turn into a white dwarf and consume some planets in the process. 

This paper reports a unique scar, the metal signature of a former planetary body on a white dwarf that rotates into view as the star rotates. 

The white dwarf has fed upon it.

New in neat planetary seismology: fifty-year-old seismic records contained the signals of the Apollo 17 lunar module warming and cooling at sunrise and sunset. Congrats to the trio of authors! Imagine what we'll be able to hear with new instruments on the Moon.

Yesterday's launch event for ESA's Euclid telescope was fantastic. As a CERN scientist, it was fascinating to learn more about how Euclid will be studying Dark Matter and Dark Energy in our universe. I can't wait to see what results this mission reveals! 🌌🧪

🔭 🧪 Two teams using the James Webb Space Telescope peered across space and time to learn secrets of strangely bright GN-z11 and its enormous black hole. GN-z11 is one of the youngest and most distant galaxies ever observed: webbtelescope.pub/3IiTZtG

Ganymede is the largest moon in our entire solar system! One of the Galilean moons, it is a satellite of Jupiter, and it is even larger than Pluto and Mercury. It's surface is quite icy, and the grooves and trenches on the surface suggest moving ice plates.

Photo taken from Juno probe in 2021

One way to answer the question: Where is the center of the Universe?

You are the center.

Because you're not measuring the entire Universe, but the Observable Universe, which necessarily is centered on the observer.

And you cannot ever know if there's anything beyond 46.5 Bly.

The gravitational waves measured by IPTA have wavelengths ~10 light-years.  TEN LIGHT-YEARS! It is literally a galactic scale observatory! Locally (on Earth scales), these GWs don't look like waves, so LIGO can't detect them. You need a galactic observatory for these. WOW.

The James Webb Space Telescope may have found that an exoplanet 124 lightyears away, K2-18b, has water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. The astronomers won't want to overstate but I'm here to tell you: that means that that planet smells like cabbage.

The "red sky paradox" (if most Earth-sized planets orbit red dwarf stars, why don't we?) is slowly but surely evaporating away, just like the atmosphere of the red-dwarf-Earths. Most of these "potentially habitable" worlds are likely barren, atmosphere-free rocks, similar to the Moon.

EXPLOSION FINGERS ✨

A feature seen by JWST in Orion 1300 light years away, these fingers are actually shocked molecular gas rushing away from a past explosion. The pink color indicates molecular hydrogen. If you look closely, you’ll see some hot iron gas in green. 🔭🧪🐡

A megalithic monument in Spain that's older than the Pyramids was recently uncovered from its watery hiding place by a drought. 

The "Spanish Stonehenge" is about 7,000 years old, some 2,000 years older than Stonehenge itself.

The Moundville Duck Bowl, a stunning example of Indigenous Mississippian culture and craft, carved from a single piece of stone. Discovered in the early 1900s in Alabama. Made in the 13th or 14th C. 📷 Moundville Archaeological Park

This is the oldest statue in London. Carved in the 2nd century, this effigy of Minerva was rediscovered in the 19th century when the head of King Alfred was added of Coade stone, and  it was installed in the garden at Trinity Church Sq in 1826.

Thamugadi in Algeria was designed to be home to 10,000 retired legionaries, but soon outgrew its original allocation.Because of the remarkable state of preservation of the ruins & the unique example of Roman urban planning with clearly delineated grids, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site

It's a year ago since I fulfilled a lifelong ambition to visit the Bronze Age site of Akrotiri on Santorini. It's a truly extraordinary place. Buildings destroyed in a volcanic eruption c. 1600 BC still stand, some up to 3 storeys high, many with their contents. 🏺

A series of camels carved into desert rock faces in north-western Saudi Arabia are actually prehistoric, dating from 7,000-8,000 years ago - before either the Pyramids of Giza or Stonehenge were built.

In addition to the oldest examples of basketry known in Europe, dating to 9500 BCE, la Cueva de los Murcielagos in Spain, is also home to the oldest known pair of shoes in Europe! (Photo Credit: MUTERMUR project). #Archaeology 🏺

A childlike and rather terrifying cupid pipes to Heracles in the House of Marcus Lucretius, Pompeii.

Dendera Temple, Egypt. Relief depicts Cleopatra & her son with Julius Caesar Caesarion.

Twelve Roman amphorae lost in a shipwreck off the coast of the Mediterranean island of Gozo 2100+ years ago; they then lay on the seabed until underwater archaeology brought them to the surface in 1961.

Syracuse, Sicily: one of my favourite Greek cities. Herewith, the Temple of Athena (built into the cathedral), the theatre, the vast altar of Hieronymous & Dionysius’ ear. Wonderful remains in one of the biggest, richest and most powerful Greek cities in the ancient world

The Roman Emperor Caligula had built two massive, elaborate palace-ships on Italy's Lake Nemi, the larger and more elaborate of which featured marble structures, mosaic floors, heating, plumbing, and a full Roman bath facility.

Archaeologists in Germany have unearthed a sword from a Bronze Age burial, and the weapon is in such good condition that it still gleams. The 3,000-year-old sword, discovered in the town of Nördlingen in Bavaria, was found in the burial of a man, woman and child.

Agrigento today is a beautiful place - a hill town with gelateria, baroque churches & its famous temples, but it was also home to the tyrant Phalaris who roasted victims alive in a bronze bull. It’s said the contraption was made to amplify the screams of his victims

An iron dagger was found in King Tut's tomb but the Egyptian iron age was 250 years after his death.
Outside of Egypt there was a small amount of iron work but it was only used for small jewelry and ceremonial items. It was worth more than gold.
One theory is the dagger was made out of a meteor.

The Golden Hat of Berlin is one of 4 found in Europe, all from the Bronze Age, and is also the best preserved hat among them.  These hats are actually astronomical calendars with which the seasons were calculated for the benefit of agricultural production. 🏺🏺🏺

10th century BC, Germany.

Ancient DNA is amazing, Part 4,982. DNA of 400-yr-old cow bones in Hispaniola and Mexico hints that the first cowboys in the Americas were enslaved Africans. Analysis backs up historians' belief that classic cowboy stuff like herding from horseback and using lassos were created by Africans, too.

The famous monumental statue of the Mexica (Aztec) goddess Coatilcue was buried by the Spanish invaders near the site of the destroyed Templo Mayor. Rediscovered in 1790, Antonio León y Gama made these illustrations of it for his 1792 book on the object, before it was reburied.

The hominin skull from Steinheim, Germany is around 250,000 years old. It likely belongs to an early Neandertal population, but has been the subject of debate ever since it was uncovered in a gravel quarry in 1933.

The Iron Crown of Lombardy was made in the Middle Ages. Legend maintains its central band was beaten from a nail of the True Cross. It was used in the coronation of the first Holy Roman Emperor Charlemagne on Christmas day 800 and was worn again in 1805 by Napoleon when he was crowned King of Italy.
