Around 2,200 years ago, the artist Hephaistion left his signature on a mosaic floor in the form of a piece of parchment held down with small drops of wax. This perfect imitation of a sheet of parchment, with one corner curled up, was discovered in one of the palaces at Pergamon.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

This is the oldest preserved human brain 🧠 at 2600 yrs old, found in Heslington, Yorkshire, UK in 2008.

The original owner was hanged, decapitated & the head immediately buried in mud.  Presumed ritual murder/sacrifice from the British Iron Age.

It's shrunk to 20% its original volume.

The Mosaic of Bacchus, discovered in 2009 in Saint-Laurent-d'Agny near Lyon, is thought to be the oldest known representation of the god of wine on a mosaic in Gaul. It was created around AD 10-14 and decorated the triclinium floor of a Roman domus. Bacchus is crowned with ivy and holding a thyrsus.

Visited the Billingsgate Roman House and Baths, a second century baths set in the courtyard of an urban house on the banks of the Thames. A fascinating site with well preserved/restored heating system AncientBlueSky 🏺

This is the Ashmolean Parchment, one of the oldest known love spells. Written in Coptic around 600 CE likely from Egypt, it binds Phello, son of Maure, to seek out and satisfy the desires of the caster, also listed in the text. Who cast it? Apapolo, son of Nooe.

Queer Pining: As old as time itself

Coin!

Copper issue of Shahrwaraz, the Sasanian general & governor of Egypt after 617. Based on the issues of Maurice, it shows the “emperor” surrounded by the Sasanian Sun & Crescent emblem. Mint mark ΑΛΕΞ on the reverse. 

Sasanians ruled in Egypt at least until 628, but probably as late as 630.

Though not much survives, the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus must have been a literally awesome sight. 

Here’s a reconstruction from National Geographic showing the whole city (left) and a closer view from DK.

A 16th century German 'oath skull' (a human skull on which defendants swore their oath in Vehmic courts) engraved with the 'magical' Roman 'Sator square', mysterious palindromic word-squares found across the Roman world, comprising the words SATOR, AREPO, TENET, OPERA, ROTAS

The undisturbed knot, before the burial chamber of King Tut, Tutankhamen, son of Akhenaten, the pariah Pharaoh who introduced Monotheism.

3300 years of time passed between his passing, and discovery.

The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian, known for the Colosseum, is also more humbly associated with public toilets in Italy and France, known as Vespasiano and Vespasienne. This association arose as a result of a tax he imposed upon the collection of urine. 

ancientbluesky 🏺

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

A man is shown worshiping a donkey-headed god on a crucifix.

Text: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

For EpigraphyTuesday: the funerary inscription of Julia Peiris, an obstetrix (midwife) who 'did no harm.' Notably, the first T of obstetrix is shoehorned in between the S and E. Dated to the 1st century CE, from Trier. Now in the Rheinisches Landesmuseum Trier.

2,300 years old, this astonishingly life-like bronze head of Thracian king Seuthes III ~ with alabaster & glass paste eyes (bloodshot!), copper strip eyelashes & eyebrows, & maybe evidence of old injury to cheekbone (Nat Mus Archaeology, Sofia) Photo: Krasimir Georgiev

Using paleoproteomics (ZooMS), my team identified a tiny hominin skull fragment at Riparo Bombrini (Level M4), in the Balzi Rossi site complex in NW Italy. Its age and cultural association material of the layer (43.5k cal. years BP, Mousterian tools) mean it's very likely a Neanderthal. 🏺🧪

Temple of Vesta (Aedes Vestae) housed the sacred fire sometimes called Iliaci foci (hearth of Ilium). 

Ovid referred to Vesta in his Fasti vi. 265 as Vesta Iliaca. There was a strong connection between the myth of Rome’s Trojan origin and the flame.

🏺Ancientbluesky

Agrippina the Elder, mother of Caligula. 
Born in Athens, she hailed from the Julio-Claudian dynasty. 
She dressed her son in traditional soldier’s attire including the caliga from which he would derive his name and be known to the annals of history: Caligula.

This relief, part of the Sebasteion at Aphrodisias is thought to depict a personification of the Roman emperor crowned by a personification of the Roman people (or the Senate or both!). The corona civica was awarded for saving the life of a citizen.

This 1,700-year-old blue glass bowl, a little more than 8 inches in diameter, was found buried next to a woman at the center of a cemetery at the Roman settlement of Emona in present-day Ljubljana in Slovenia.  Its Greek inscription reads: "Drink to live forever, for many years!"

Stacked Roman amphora fragments at Monte Testaccio in #Rome, essentially an ancient dumping ground for unwanted vessels (reusing not being a notable phenomenon) - these are just a small number of up to 53 MILLION vessels abandoned here in antiquity!

Koi Krylgan Kala is an archeological site from Karakalpakstan Republic. Built by Iranian people from the kingdom of Khwarezm (a sister civilization of a perhaps more famous Sogdia) in 4 cent BCE. Koi Krylgan Kala was both a fortress, a settlement, and a Zoroastrian temple.

The Roman wall surrounding the city of Lugo in Galicia is the only complete Roman wall remaining. According to legend the Romans built it to protect a forest and not a city, the“Sacred Forest of Augusto”. The walls still contain 85 towers and have survived intact for 18 centuries.
ancientbluesky 🏺

Thonis-Heraklion was founded in C8thBCE at the Nile’s mouth. Before Alexandria’s creation, it was the major port of the delta. Succeeding earthquakes & floods saw the city slip under the waves in C2ndBCE eventually disappearing c.900CE. It has been excavated since 1933
📷Franck Goddio🏺 AncientBlueSky

While Greek and Roman statuary is often seen as originating the 'naturalistic' tradition in Europe, both the idealized statues of the Greek archaic period and the more naturalistic Hellenistic style have precedents in Egyptian art

The forum and Byzantine fort in Madauros, Algeria. It became a Roman colony for veterans in the 1st century. It was the birthplace in 124AD of the Numidian Apuleius who wrote the only Latin Roman novel, "The Golden Ass", to survive intact. St Augustine of Hippo also studied there.

Ancientbluesky 🏺

I had never heard of the Temple of Olympian Zeus at Akragas (Agrigento), but it was apparently the largest Doric temple ever built (though maybe it was never finished?). The "Atlas" figures are thought to be defeated enemies. Much of it now makes up the local dock.

The colossal bronze statue of Hercules that stands in the Vatican Museums. The 13-foot statue was unearthed in Rome in 1864, under a slab inscribed 'FCS' - Fulgor Conditum Summanium, indicating that the statue had been ritually buried after being struck by lightning.

This incredible bronze cuirass was found in Maresse, France. It was made as the Bronze Age was transitioning to the Iron Age & dates to the C9th/8thBCE. Along with several others, it was given as a votive offering. Give it a whopping wow, please

A startlingly beautiful image of an ancient Egyptian female acrobat painted on a c. 3,250 year old limestone potsherd.

Late Bronze Age baby-feeding vessels from Vösendorf, Austria, about 3000 years old. They have a tiny hole in the tail, so you can fill them with milk, and then an infant would hold it, and suck the milk through the tail. Ingenious and so pretty! Photo: Enver-Hirsch / Wien Museum

Discovered that the carpal tunnel I got, is exacerbated by a previously unnoticed upper arm/shoulder RSI. Started doing the basic stretches they taught back in gym class and- it feels instantly better and its finally improving again.

One of the best and laziest things you can do for your shoulder and upper back health is resistance band pull aparts. You can do a ton of them, all day long, toss em into any empty spaces in workouts, etc. Impossible to do too many, can do em anywhere anytime.

Make it a stim, and you're golden.

#ZigHealthTip If you're trying to lose weight (or gain it), do your best to weigh yourself daily. Why? Because your bodyweight can fluctuate by as much as FIFTEEN POUNDS from water retention. Daily weigh-ins allow you to extract averages. Log the weight, plot it on a graph and watch the trend.

Fitness, strength, muscle building, and power secret: 

You could do literally nothing but the following exercises ever again and benefit for literally the entire rest of your life:

- squat
- lunge
- deadlift/RDL
- hip thrust
- bench (or push-up if you like)

There are so many things I’ve learned in the last 2 years since I started getting fit (and hench) that I wish someone had told me when I was young. One of them is that the muscle soreness you get for a week after you work out a muscle group pretty much only happens the first time you do it

#ZigHealthTip: In the immortal words of Lee Haney, “Simulate, don’t annihilate.” Your goal with every gym session should be to do enough work to adequately stimulate muscle growth. If you’re frying your muscles so much that you’re sore for 2 weeks, that’s too much. Ease back.

pro gym tip: when feeling lazy, completely lowball your goal for the day and then get caught in a "well i'm close to X, i might as well keep going a little more" and "this song's a banger, i'll stop after" loop to double or triple your time in the gym. money moves right there.

Weight lifting has really changed the way I think about food/nutrition for the better. Before, my idea of healthy eating was all about reduction--eating as little as possible without collapsing, really. Now I think of it in terms of nourishing myself, giving myself strength. It rules.

I've written a lot on Twitter about people beginning strength training, and all of it can be condensed to:

Pick any reputable program you like the look of that actually fits into your life, and do what it says for 6 weeks. 

Then advice will be useful. Before then it won't be.

re back injury: if u lift weights and take a break bc of covid, take waaaay longer to recover before returning than you think you need; im positive the reason my back got messed up is bc i started lifting again too soon. there is likely still a ton of inflammation in ur bod even though u feel normal

I will say - aiming to eat 3500 calories a day has made getting to 2500 daily a breeze. I often fall short of the goal, but I don't think I've missed minimums since setting that goal. And I don't get nearly as bad next day pain from the gym any more and it's only been like 2 weeks.

You can do the stuff you want to and you don't really have to do the stuff you hate.

Women too. Especially women.

The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple, meaning "the Great God of the Cave", is the largest and most ornate Hindu temple in the medieval temple group found at Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, India. It is considered one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India.

Bramante’s Tempietto, door from inside.
Donato Bramante, Tempietto, c. 1502, San Pietro in Montorio, Rome. The “Tempietto” or little temple is a martyia (a building that commemorates a martyrdom) that marks the traditional site of Saint Peter’s crucifixion.

Blue-winged Mountain Tanagers are common, widespread tanagers found in the Andean cloud forest. Their favorites food are insects, fruits, and small berries. And yes, this bird is a stunner. It is unmistakable with its bright yellow, black, and electric blue coloring. 🪶

Ladder-backed Woodpeckers (Dryobates scalaris) are native to the SW US and MX. They can be inconspicuous and quiet, requiring a bit of time and patience to find. Their small size and agility make them deft foragers.

Woodpecker Wednesday! 🪶🌿

The Common Kingfisher has to be one of the UK's most beautiful birds: it looks like it belongs in the tropics rather than fishing along a stream in a wee London park next to a children's playground, but that's where I found this handsome chap this morning in Colliers Wood. 🪶

A Great Blue Heron from the Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska (last month). An everyday bird for many of you, well those in North America, but we are at the very edge of the range and lucky to get a sighting a year.

A stunning Scarlet-Bellied Mountain Tanager working on some feather care - the bird was happily bathing. These fruit eating birds can be found in the high forests of the Andes Mountains from Venezuela to Bolivia. 🌿🧪🪶🐡
Zuroloma Birding, Nono, Ecuador

On the Hunt - Northern Water Shrew 🦋 
The North American Water Shrew is our smallest diving mammal at only 14-16cm long. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting in the pitch black of rivers and ponds by using its long whiskers to feel and blowing air bubbles which it then inhales to smell for prey.

The pyrrhuloxia or desert cardinal is a bird that inhabits the arid region from the southwestern United States to central Mexico.
It is similar to the northern cardinal in terms of song and behavior, and both coexist in many desert areas.

Gang-gang Cockatoos! These endangered beauties are roughly the size of Galahs and limited to a small area of coastal South-Eastern Australia. The local flock visited our garden yesterday, and I counted *sixteen* of them!

The great white pelican also known as the eastern white pelican, rosy pelican or simply white pelican is a bird in the pelican family. 
            It breeds from southeastern Europe through Asia and Africa, in swamps and shallow lakes.

We have been hosting gracklepalooza at our place as more than a hundred had gathered in preparation for their journey south. Most have moved on but we still have quite a few. This male common grackle was foraging in the trees  and really showing off the colours!

The Kolea are back! After flying for 8000 miles or so down from the arctic, an entire flock (about 50 or so) arrived at Kealia Pond NWR last week. Pacific golden plovers are amazing....this one still showing some breeding plumage. :) 🦉📷🌿

🦅📉 Arizona's bald eagle population was reduced to three pairs in 1970! 😮‍💨

Due to elimination of DDT, habitat protection, & artificial reproduction efforts there were 69 breeding pairs, & a record 87 young bald eagles hatched during 2019. 📈

#ArtAdventCalendar Day 5: Bald Eagle, Lynx Lake, AZ 🪶🌿🐡

The jetties at Playa del Rey are the best place in Los Angeles to see surfbirds, including this one starting to molt into its summer plumage yesterday. In a few months, they will depart for Alaska and the Yukon 🪶📷

Wonky winter wildlife! For some reason this Gray Catbird decided to stay on the north shore of Lake Ontario this winter. It was chowing down on invasive European Buckthorn berries which are aplenty so I guess it's doing well enough. 🪶

If you love birds, you need to conserve bugs. In this BugShot - Birding crossover event we have a Lemon-browed Flycatcher with a katydid. The bird say there with it so it was probably waiting for us to leave so it could head to its nest. 🌿🪶#inverts

For many of you a common bird but White-throated Sparrows are quite rare in #Alaska. This one has been spending the winter in Anchorage. Same spot as there was one last winter but I didn't get a picture to compare. 🪶

What a day! Visited Last Mountain Lake Migratory Bird Sanctuary (Saskatchewan, Canada) to spend a day watching the greatest show on earth. Saw about 30 bird species, and easily 50,000 individuals. Geese, ducks, cranes, harriers, eagles... 🪶

Great Blue Heron taking a 17-minute bath in a lagoon next to San Francisco Bay, in four parts. 
1. He floats lazily about. 
2. He sits down in water. 
3. He dives in and shakes himself off. 
4. He flies away, probably to nearby pine tree.

🧪One of the coolest critters I’ve ever seen, an aptly named rhinoceros katydid (Copiphora rhinoceros) spotted in the Monteverde area of Costa Rica. I’ve never been much of an entomology gal, but this guy sold me 🦗

Most woodpeckers are black and white. This is true in South America too, but the Crimson Mantled Woodpecker breaks that trend. They are considered common, but this summer was the first time I saw one. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Learning the ropes as a young Red-tailed Hawk is challenging - annual survival to one year is about 36% - this youngster is likely looking at a gray squirrel (which is tricky prey) running up a tree & hiding - a bit baffled, the hawk circled the tree & then looked off into the distance 🪶🌿

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

Yellow -rumped warblers are among the first warblers to arrive back after their winter vacation.

Some underwater critters from Saturday out at Tāwharanui. You'll see spotties, snapper, goat fish, sweep, hiwihiwi and a few eagle rays, one feeding. Also, a lot of attempts to capture the beautiful colours of the seaweed, the ground and the sponges. #MarineLife 🦑 #Kikorangi #Aotearoa

Bird Glamour’s Glamager celebrates the Summer Tanager! This southern US to Central American bright red bird is both beautiful and deadly. Summer Tanagers specialize in eating bees and wasps, which they catch mid-flight and then beat to death by whacking their catch against a branch! 🐦 🐡

While looking for squirrel pictures I was reminded of one of my favorite warbler photos of all time. Audubon's Yellow-rumped Warbler in a tangle of dead and weathered branches. I found this little guy at Lassen National Park. The Audubon's form is mostly found in mountainous western North America. 🪶

A snazzy Golden-naped Tanager pausing for a photo. These birds came be found in the Andean cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. They are omnivorous and dine on fruit and insects. 🌿🧪🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Hello Bluesky. Here's the 'Type Specimen' art for my favourite bird, the Yellow-throated Scrubwren, from my soon to be published 'Little Brown Birds of the Sydney Region'. This bird has a fascinating, feeding and breeding ecology with many exciting mutualisms and commensalisms. #birds #birding

Welcome to the #BirdGlamour Glamager series, starting with the Western Tanager from western North America! The Western Tanager is extra and DOES NOT get his red coloration from a diet rich in carotenoids like many birds do: their red comes from rhodoxanthin from their insect-rich diet. 🐡🐦

Western meadowlark in front of a great egret over a vernal pool!

IIRC there is a law of economics that says "in a perfect monopoly, prices will rise to the maximum amount the market will bear". I've always argued that, in most cases, prices will do that anyway.
A lot of people had more money after COVID, so the prices went up. 
Greed *IS* the system.

The cost of not enacting a wealth tax is far greater than enacting one. They're already buying politicians, judges, journalists, laws, etc. Most of the money they're using for it comes from the windfall tax savings from the Reagan revolution and subsequent Bush and Trump tax cuts.

You haven’t seen true ego lifting until you’ve been to a Vegas gym. Simultaneous double bicep curls with weights that are WAY too heavy. Partial reps only. Rotate like 5 degrees at most. And as fast as you can go. Then drop the weights. Good. All the ladies are impressed. By how tiny your reps are.

“If you are looking for a different way to observe Lent, you could combine your practice with yoga practice. Lent in 29 yoga postures.”- the advert says. 

Let me just tell you, there are Catholic priests that would spontaneously combust if they heard about this.

The sad room that passes for a fitness center in my building is a joke, but a mostly-unilateral leg day is not. Focusing on single leg movements highlights imbalances & allows for a serious workout with relatively low weight. Doing supersets keeps it manageable. Workout below:

In my yoga practice, I'm familiar with instructors talking about the balance between effort and ease, and (maybe not surprisingly) I'm much more of an effort person. But today at church, I felt the ease of this vocation in a way I don't usually, which was a good feeling.

i am a light vinaigrettes guy:

champagne vinegar, olive oil, meyer lemon juice, black pepper, thyme, and some good dijon mustard shaken together is one of my go-to dressings.

also don’t forget to salt your salad!

A salad hot (correct) take: 

If you are making a salad with kale, cranberries, goat cheese, and candied walnuts, do not under any circumstances also make the dressing sweet. Goat cheese is not enough savory/tangy on its own to deal with all that. This salad is like eating the saddest candy bar :(

the aldi find of the week was crispy “dutch style” fried onions. starting to think the secret to scandinavian success isn’t their weird flavors of market socialism, nor their dense urban cores, nor their precarious oil wealth. what if it’s all just giant tubes of fried onions for a buck and change

This dish isn’t terribly photogenic, which is a shame because it’s seriously delicious so I hope you’ll give it a chance. It’s a Greek dish called gigantes plaki. Dried lima beans are baked in a garlicky tomato sauce until they are velvety soft and divinely flavorful.

Pappardelle coccolare, or Coddle pasta in Hiberno English. You need a good few fatty rashers and sausages here. Make a normal carbonara sauce w two extra egg yolks for a thicker sauce and some sharp white Dubliner cheese grated in. give it a good mix. Parsley to garnish and white pepper to serve.

Friends, chili crunch is waiting to help you fill the sriracha mayo-sized hole in your heart. 

Or maybe it’s just time for a new hot love. 

Anyhoo, Calabrian chili crunch and mayo = bangin’ bacon sammich coming right up. 🍽️🍜

Potatoes are nutritionally complete. In addition to starch, potatoes contain vitamins, minerals, and fiber. They're rich in vitamin C, which is an antioxidant…

…I tell myself, reheating my 4th baked potato of the day.

kind of lukewarm on a lot of the American Culinary Contributions to the world but the sliced everything bagel with a sausage patty, flat-top fried egg, and cheddar cheese wrapped in foil is an all-timer

The best "bad" meal I know how to make is a bag of frozen ravioli, a big jar of Prego, and a heap of low-moisture Mozzarella. Lightly grease an oven safe something, mix the frozen ravioli and sauce, smother with cheese, and bake at ~350 until bubbly. Shake parmesan to taste. It's stellar at effort

Got my pork on the grill and it'll be going all day.

I made a spritz bottle filled with apple juice, ginger juice, apple cider vinegar, and a touch of orange juice that I'll be spraying on this meat every hour or so to flavour and moisten it.

Look how fucking juicy this roast came out goddamn

It’s cooked medium rare, too, that’s just the result of dry aging and reverse searing; it’s been roasting all day

(With roast potatoes, seared broccoli, and a horseradish crème fraiche)

Omelette stuffed w squash blossoms, tomatillos, shiitakes, cheese spread (grated cheddar mixed w herbed feta). Seasoned w rosemary, paprika, black truffle salt. The sliced avocado has scorpion salt (yes, real scorpions) #FoodIsGood

The cafeteria at my work offer basic af cheeseburgers. But I transform them by going to salad bar to grab dressings, blue cheese, pickled onions, jalapeño slices and fried shallots as additional toppings 🤌🤌

Cut up your jack-o-lantern, throw your spice rack (and a little brown sugar) at it, roast it, and then put a nice hanger steak on top. Simple, satisfying and yet at the same time a provocative mashup of various old and new worlds. Also just a nice break from potatoes. Eat them pumpkins!

Baked at 400 for 15 minutes in a glass baking dish, with "icing" of mayo, lemon, pepper, and chopped garlic greens. old fishboat recipe, one of my fave quick ways to cook fish.

the salad nothing fancy - the end of a romaine heart, jarred dressing, bacon bits, grated Grana Padano

🍜

This was not the prettiest omelette. My pan needs to be more non stick. It was however maybe the best omelette I’ve ever eaten and that is because the mushrooms were cooked in a splash of aged balsamic and a shit tonne of butter and the omelette was cooked in even more butter.

🌱 The pink bumblebee is here. You can see some pink pigmentation on this fruit but this is still mostly orange. As it ripens the color will change to more pink strains.  This was the best tasting tomato I grew last year.

My primary reason for being on Madeira was hoping to encounter one of the rarest native European orchids, Madeiran endemic Goodyera macrophylla. Only a few dozen plants are known to flower annually, and in dangerously inaccessible places. Seeing this plant was a moment of sheer euphoria.

Despite the chilly cold front this week, new patches of toothleaf goldeneyes along the Salado Greenway went in for a fall bloom! These attractive asters grow in bunched colonies and can be up to 3-6 ft tall. I think I saw three or four patches in flower while out on my run yesterday. 🌼🌿

🌱 The bosque blue tomatoes are starting to ripe. The bottom of ripe tomatoes turn from greenish to a red hue. The top part remains the dark side of blue violet, about the color of eggplant. These are larger than the strawberry blk cherry tomatoes, but the taste is kind of unmemorable.

Watermelon seedling finding a new home in thick organic mulch, a mixture of dried grass with cow and donkey manure.

We are doing a trial to determine whether the manure is broken down enough to support seedling growth.

If it is, and we get fruits, they going be delicious! 🌱

Two years ago, I planted passion flower seeds, but they didn't germinate — due to weather conditions. One seed found its way to the base of a small tree and sprouted. The vine began climbing the tree. This year the tree is covered with passion flowers and passion fruit. A delightful turn of events!

🌱 I'd like to pin this on my hair! 

This is the apricot lemonade cosmos. The flowers can change to become more pink than yellow at various times, or the other way around. Very muted pale colors that looks particularly lovely when grown next to lavender and dahlias.

My other half got this “Waxed Amaryllis” at Trader Joe’s. It grew as promised & is in full bloom. It's lovely, but I hate “disposable” plants. I’m going to try to liberate it from its plastic jacket, get it into a pot, & save it. #Hippeastrum ‘Christmas Gift’ #Amaryllidaceae 🌱

This summer I grew alpine strawberries for the first time. Not only did they taste much more delicious than my regular strawberries, they were easier to grow from seed and produced constantly from spring to the last frost. I got a far bigger harvest from these than from my regular ones. 🌱🍓

🌱The Mexican sunflowers are just starting to bloom next to the lavender at the neighbor's farm. The flowers are small like daisies, and usually abundant. Not as tall as the perennial sunflowers I'm growing the last two years.

Happy New Year! Hoping for something completely different? How about Passiflora foetida the Stinking Passionflower? This one was blooming at the base of a heiau at La Perouse Bay, Jan. 2019. Certainly one of nature’s weirder floral arrangements!Wishing everyone a fun new year, maybe not this weird.🌿

🌱 The "bosque blue" tomato is shaping up but compared to the black strawberry cherry tomato I grew last year, there is no shade of blue here, just a dark, almost black color. Have not tasted it yet but will likely grow the blk starwberry again instead of the bb next year.

Native to the Mediterranean, Oregano loves to grow in sunny Florida! Give it a big spot in well-drained soil and it will flourish. After flowering the plant dies back some, but can still withstand a hearty trimming. Prune to a small bush as it can get leggy and try to spread. 🌱

📷: Tanria Elementals

🌱 Of all the volunteer tomatoes I have this year, this is the most surprising. This is a Black Strawberry Cherry tomato growing by my garden gate which has mostly crushed rocks. I did not plant this variety this year but here it is!

🌱 The wild columbine is blooming all over the garden. A native which is not planted so much as exuberantly spreads itself by seeds. Not hugely invasive and easy to pull up if it lands somewhere you really don't want it but a lovely early spring bloom to enjoy while waiting for other plants to bloom.

Papaver somniferum (Opium Poppy), love her crinkled petals when she has just emerged.  These grow well in my garden and I just scatter the seed in situ.  They are some of my favourite blooms.  🌱

#GardeningFeed🌱
#Flowers #DollysCottageGarden

Decided to plant potatoes on a whim in an overgrown area of the property surrounded by hedges. Had no idea if it got enough light or had good enough soil. Of all 3 areas I planted them, it’s flourishing best here. Sometimes rolling the dice pays off. 🌱

yellow jessamine | Gelsemium sempervirens 🌿

one of the great plants of the deep south, but it only gets into the southeastern corner of Virginia, and is not common.

After a month of searching the Appomattox valley, I found enough seed for my friend to try to grow at her local native plant nursery.

A lovely old French iris from 1927, 'Floridor'. It has a wonderful, heavy grape fragrance, but its best feature is the unique shade of blue, often described as slate blue in the old catalogs. It really stands out in the garden and is such a good grower and bloomer.

We have a ridiculous volume of California bluebell seeds and the temptation to seed-bomb with them is pretty high. Who wouldn’t love them? Good foliage, they die back completely to make room for summer blooms, and that BLUE—it’s unmatched 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Siberian Iris Paprikash is one of the stranger Siberian Iris I have. The flower color is decidedly different. Goes well with other colors in the garden. Like other Iris relatives it prefers full sun and tolerates dry when established. Not picky about soil. 🌱

🌱 Eastern black swallowtail/ Papilio polyxenes asterius (Stoll) finally alights on a red dahlia/ Dahlia parvibracteata (Saar & P.D. Sørensen) so I can take her picture. 🦋
Black swallowtails, pipevine swallowtails, and monarchs, as well as many bees favor this nonnative to my area single dahlia.

The alpine strawberries we uncovered when rescuing the patio from under the rubble and detritus have been absolute stars. Fruit every few days for almost two months now, feels like they are saying “thank you!” (Or possibly: “oh heck we need to spread seed everywhere before that happens again”) 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Check seed packet date ranges prior to starting seedlings.  Some vegetable seeds germinate quickly and grow just as fast.  Too often I hear from people, "Oh, I started my tomatoes in January and now they're all leggy." Yes, wait, read directions, don't start too soon. 🌱

More signs of spring:
1) daffodils really putting it out now
2) hellebores (lenten rose)
3) flowering quince
4) apricot blossom (peach blossoms should be blooming soon; later pear & tart cherry, then last are the apple blossoms)

Last year’s dwarf irises—among my very favourite plants—were a treasure. This year we learn whether they’ll perennialize in the spots I picked for them, but as insurance, we planted another fifty. There can never be too many! They take up no space; their post-bloom foliage is narrow and subtle 🌱

Many micro-farmers will tell you that their most profitable crops are salad greens. Think about how much you pay for a bag of greens that lasts you a couple weeks and makes a few salads.

Growing your own greens is fun, easy, and saves $$$!

🥬 greens 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

Corn plant propagation going well now that I've added an aquarium pump to the vases. Propagation station looking healthy. The schefflera, on the other hand, is getting cramped. Should have pruned it months ago. 🌱

Tired: Harvard Extension School isn't a prestigious program, it's basically the same education you'd get at the local community college

Wired: also, Harvard University is basically the same education you'd get at the local community college

No one should be surprised by the mass abandoning of DEI efforts because most NEVER understood, cared about or bought into a reality that acknowledges and seeks to repair the inequity and harm resulting from systems, institutions, and policies DESIGNED to privilege the few at the expense of the many

All of these DEI initiatives, while mostly well intentioned, will ultimately fail because they are beholden to the systems in which they operate. It is also true that conservatives are antagonistic to the ideas of equity and inclusion in institutions valued by society.

Reports of the death of DEI are greatly exaggerated. In fact, I just heard that one PE firm is requiring all its portfolio companies to implement DEI goals and reporting. They know DEI means better decisions and better results.

If you major in whatever is the very best thing for Mississippi, will it be free? No?

Politicians fund universities at single-digit percentages of their expenses but nevertheless retain fine-grained control over who gets to choose to learn which things, and that’s not a fair ask

the implicit underlying premise of every argument against affirmative action and DEI is "there is an objective, rank-ordered, consistent hierarchy of excellence, the top of the list is white guys, and jumping the line is the worst possible affront, because you're clearly sacrificing excellence"

Imagine being one of the richest universities in the world and advertising PhD-requiring fellowships for <£28k. And then saying you care about equality & diversity amongst academics. I hesitate to even post these on the jobs wiki, it’s so embarrassing (and the jobs are obscure and badly explained)

It’s incredibly depressing to hear talk of how we need *more* business people involved in leading nonprofit institutions of higher education, as their goals & experience are often fundamentally misaligned with the missions of universities. “Doesn’t guarantee success” is a dramatic understatement.

I’ve talked about this a lot from the stage and mic

DEI IS NOT an effort of finding individuals who sit at multiple intersectional identities and calling that a win

DEI is about HIRING and, more importantly, RETAINING a workforce that and helps…

Turning most faculty into adjuncts (which never bugged the tenured class) was the wedge that creeps like Gordon Gee and Ben Sasse are using to gut and financialize the rest of the university. It always goes back to how the bosses handle Labor. There's a reason every institution hates unions.

West Virginia's conservative government flushing WVU down the drain for no real reason is a good reminder that the causation arrow of "economically backwards place develops reactionary politics" is often the opposite of how people assume it is.

Seeing chatter in my blog comments about nutty college profs and I'm struck by a few things: 

1) Examples tend to come from elite private schools. 70% of students are at publics, but apparently the great majority of goofballs teach at privates. 

Public universities are better.

I just can't read this. I'm at chronically, severely under-funded CUNY b/c the gov has decided we're not important enough to warrant the investment. CUNY is the opposite of "elite", & I'm proud of it. We educate and move more 1st gen students  into the middle class than all the Ivies combined.

What really blows my mind is that the universities that do well in REF get more money than those that don't.  So it's a self-fulfilling prophecy every REF. Oh, the place that got more research funding did more research?!?? WOW

Can we do a connection of what happened at the Grace Hopper conférence to how most of the DEI positions went to white women

And how the failure to talk about that has hampered all real progress in real moderation and resource building for gender representation

As someone who has served on many a task force + strategic planning committee whose work goes nowhere beyond the final presentation-to-admin, suggesting that the whole enterprise was merely a means of performing institutional commitment to faculty governance: I hear this 👍

It was always obvious that the Conservative government’s concern wasn’t really about academic freedom or free speech on campus, but about protecting certain kinds of speech from certain academics, while creating the conditions for further government intervention in higher education.

I know a lot of people are just working off of templates, but I think it's always worth thinking about whether the job listing you are creating actually requires a college education and why you think it does because I would bet that very few of them actually do.

huh. that’s weird. the article itself ends up implying that, while things are absolutely better, market forces alone are not remedy for problems that are intrinsically sociopolitical in nature, such as biased employment environments and inherent access to opportunity. what is a term for this? DEI?

Re last reskeet: whatever the truth of the economics I have been *explicitly* told by UK uni management that the measure of my success as an academic is the number of taught postgraduate students i can recruit as they are the “only thing that makes money” (yes, grants no longer count as profit!)

not to sound like an anti-institutionalist but it's really hard to overstate how thoroughly people with elite positions of influence in trendy disciplines at expensive universities have revealed themselves to be utterly amoral, craven sycophants concerned solely with their personal material wealth

An essay on what's happening at WVU that you absolutely must not miss --

Emily Hilliard, former WV state folklorist, makes the connections between the West Virginia teachers' strikes five years ago and the the student organizing at WVU right now. 

Lessons were learned, and that's also education.

One of the most frustrating things about academia is the longer I’m in it, the more I know via direct experience or friends experiences about how many academics who present themselves as the kindest, most inclusive mentors are the biggest bullies to junior colleagues.

Companies have always been expected to train their employees, the idea that is the traditional role of universities is a rancid lie that has been used to promote business degrees and slash arts and social sciences funding since the 80s

The many tenured academics who once told themselves they would take more radical positions once they got tenure are actually the ones disciplining and punishing untenured faculty for their unwavering ethical and political commitments.

I'm curious whether any law schools with DEI missions see that mission as including paying staff properly--including the essential staff without whom the school will literally not work (IT, library, AV, facilities, etc), whether or not those staff & their functions boost empty public hoopla.

One thing that irks me with DEI is the opposition to simple actions on the grounds that they are just "symbolic". Symbolic actions aren't enough, but refusing to do something just because "it's symbolic" is an admission that even no-effort actions are too much to ask. And anyway - symbols matter.

I liked Poor Things a lot: it captures the topsy-turviness of Alasdair Gray's brain, his acerbic wit, and his horrors at how cruel human beings can be. Like Frankenstein, both the book & the film ask us who the real monsters are.

Saw Poor Things yesterday and thought it was an extremely interesting movie with lots to chew on about gender and identity in a patriarchal structure. The sets were amazing too, particularly all the weird hodgepodge creatures.

Just finished Poor Things (2023). I’ve seen most of Lanthimos’s previous films, and they were good, but I’ve had a bit of trouble connecting with them. I had no such issue here. This is marvelous stuff. Visually incredible, this is one of the best looking movies I’ve seen in a quite a while.

PASSAGES (streaming on Mubi) is a really well-done, excruciating slow burn film about the messiness of relationships — especially when they begin falling apart. 

It’s 90 mins of beautiful people having sex and/or behaving terribly. Just know that you’ll never look at Paddington the same way again

One of my favourite "vibes" horror films. There is a plot and characters but none of that matters. What matters is the terrifying goblin score and gorgeous lighting, dreamlike editing. It's a rather beautiful nightmare made real.

Also watched Talk To Me, which was excellent. Really creative and dread-inducing small scale horror. Performances were outstanding and the ghost scenes are grotesquely creative. It’s an A24 movie, so you know you’re getting something unusual and high quality.

Love Lies Bleeding was everything I hoped it would be. One of the absolute best modern sapphic films, equally hot, passionate, toxic and uncomfortable. It's complex and messy and never shies away from taking the most visceral punch possible. Absolutely loved it, best film I've seen this year.

Looking for something unique? Check out TETSUO: THE IRON MAN

Likened to the work of David Lynch and David Cronenberg, TETSUO molds explosive violence, bizarre sexual imagery and jet-black humor into a cinematic experience unlike any you've seen.

My favorite movie from this year about a naïve woman who goes on an adventure around the Real World with a swarthy himbo companion learning harsh lessons about Patriarchy and Feminism before returning home and creating the life she wants for herself? It's called POOR THINGS

All of us Strangers is probably the most mature and nuanced gay film about adult romance and trauma to come out of North America in…forever?  But it didn’t get an Oscar nom because it’s actually for gay people.

such a beautiful & elegant minute-plus long crane shot in the funhouse. tracks the little brother making his way towards the exit as stragglers clear out. captures unspoken confusion of this kid by considering space around him. this kid's experience on this night is the best stuff in the film imo

Thoroughly enjoyed You Hurt My Feelings. I’m getting old.
Smartly written comedies about people doing the best they can and aren’t trying to be edgy or push boundaries are my go to movies anymore and this is a good one. See also this year’s Asteroid City.

Talk to Me is a solid-A horror movie. Loved it. It coulda been an A+ if they had titled it Talk To The Hand… I had to shave three points off for such an obvious unforced error. Otherwise, just perfect.

Finally saw WHERE THE DEVIL ROAMS, from The Adams Family. Dark, brooding period piece about a murderous family of carnival workers with haunting visuals. Really enjoyed it. Big fan of The Adams Family's work. Coolest family working in indie genre film today. Streaming on Tubi. 🎥 #filmsky #horrorsky

Watched All the Colors of the Dark (1972) tonight… I’m not a big giallo guy, but they’re occasionally very nice as palette cleansers. Anyhow, this is one the good ones. A very paranoid film, very creepy, great soundtrack.

Messiah of Evil (1974) is one of the most beautiful films I’ve seen in a long time. I want to live in it, even if I get violently murdered. It would be a good looking death. No idea what’s happening most of the time, don’t care

THE LOST BOYS is my favorite vampire movie ever. It’s got an incredible soundtrack, the two Coreys, and some guy named Kiefer. I love Technicolor films. Say what you want about Shumacher and his wild aesthetics. He sure nailed this one. Sometimes it’s better to stay off the boardwalk.🩸

THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I’ve seen all year. It’s like Linklater’s THE WRESTLER. Whether you know the story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you’ll likely be as blown away as I was.

THANKSGIVING: Tawdry, icky and a truly indecent amount of fun. So nice, amidst the current vogues of Elevated Horror and PG-13 entry-level stuff (both producing excellent work!), to see a good old-fashioned slasher. There’s a real sicko glee at work here and thank goodness.

The World's Great Sinner (1962)

Absolutely batty film about an insurance salesman who gets canned from his job and becomes a cult leader

Starring, written, and directed by Timothy Carey, BOTD in 1929

Not only is this one of the best horror movies I’ve ever seen, it’s one of the best movies I’ve ever seen. You have to watch The Shining and Doctor Sleep back to back for that PUNCH. It MASSIVELY improved the original, enriching the lore & not being vague abt Jacks demise. 10/10 #horrorsky #filmsky

homage to 'invitation to hell' wes craven tv movie, shot it right after he did nightmare. about a hole to hell hidden inside a country club. robert urich builds a heat proof suit to travel down inside, his family are replaced with demons

Talk to Me is hands down the best horror I’ve seen in a long time. The concept/story is intriguing. good paced storytelling. Characters are well built. Chemistry is on spot. Scare scenes make sense, not just random pointless jump scares. And I got a good chill in the end. Plus the Aussie elements 🤣👌

To BAFTA for RAGING GRACE, which is going to be one of the films of the year. Mother-daughter immigrant story that becomes a thriller that becomes racially-charged horror. And that great rarity, a British genre film. General release soon, seek it out.

📽️🍿 I thought SATAN'S SLAVES was a very effective & creepy movie, but hot damn did they upgrade the horror with COMMUNION. Intense & atmospheric, this sequel was an eerie joy to watch. Disturbing, unnerving, and filled with supremely scary shit- I highly recommend them BOTH.

📽️ filmsky horror movie

MANDY is an incredible cinematic masterpiece. The camerawork is alluring. The colors are so warm! I love that it’s done in acts. It’s a brilliant artistic film that also happens to be an awesome horror flick. The characters are engaging. The plot has some very tender moments. 📽️🕸️🍿🗃️

You *will* be moved by a horror comedy featuring a very-much-alive-yet-elderly Elvis defending his nursing home from a soul sucking mummy with the help of JFK (who is also still alive -- and black!). Can The King regain his long buried mojo and save the residents of Shady Rest? Don't bet against it.

One of the best horror films I've ever seen. A genuinely creepy premise superbly realised. Go in knowing as little as possible and prepare to have your mind blown. It has one of the most powerful opening scenes ever. And it's as Aussie as roadkill kangaroo without any cultural cringe.Just brilliant.

Listening back to that first Godspeed album, and it's still so good isn't it? Creates a whole little audio world so easily, and it's genuinely unsettling in places. Extra points for the sheer amount of post-rock bands that tried to copy it, but never picked the right bits to copy.

kate rusby’s entire catalog is back on spotify. this is my favorite song of hers. pay attention to the guy repeating the flute melody in the right ear, the earnestness with which she tells this funny story, and the magical ending that i won’t spoil

the strokes are such a mid band but their production more than makes up for it making them Awesome proving once and for all that rock and roll has nothing to do with songwriting or talent but the ability to sound Cool

This is Neptune in true color. Some fun facts about Neptune: it's made mostly of hydrogen, with a lot of helium, methane, ammonia, and water mixed in. It has the fastest winds in the solar system, and it rains diamonds. Also, no homophobes live there.

A pleasing evening calculation, prepping for my Introductory Astrophysics class:  

If the Sun is orbiting the Galactic centre at the typical stellar rotation velocity (~225 km/s), how many times has the Sun been around the Galaxy during its ~5 billion year lifetime?

Answer: around 20 times 💃

🔭🧪

Just had a black hole explained in such simple terms (probably an over simplification)

The Earth's escape velocity is ~8 miles/second

Our Sun's is ~400 miles/s

A star can grow so massive that its escape velocity exceeds the speed of light. That's a black hole, light can't escape for us to see.

The milky way galaxy rotates once every 200 million years or so, and as such our solar system is traveling at roughly 143 mi/s (514,000 mph). What this means is that every second, some number of ghosts of aliens who've died in space are traveling through your body at half a million miles per hour.

The Orion Nebula (right) is about 24 light years across but only has a total mass of ~2,000 suns.

Most of the material is spread out over this vast distance making the gas very thinned out. 

That’s why (even it’s the size of two full moons) you can’t see it with naked eyes, it’s very dim. 🔭🧪

We published some new cool black hole results! The Event Horizon Telescope measured the black hole in Messier 87 again in 2018 to confirm our observations from the previous year! The ring has a remarkably similar size to our 2017 data, giving us additional confidence that it is a black hole. 🔭🧪

Quite a thrill this week to see the ancient ruins at Akrotiri, on the island of Santorini. Remains of a once thriving and wealthy civilisation, destroyed when most of the island exploded in a volcanic eruption around 1600 BC. 

#AncientSiteSunday

Currently on display at the Cluny Museum in Paris, this ancient spear head made from rock crystal was found in south west France and probably dates from the Ice Age. Although its shape is not unusual, the use of the crystal suggests it had special significance.

The Madrague de Giens is a Roman shipwreck off the coast of S France, sinking mid C1stBCE. It was a huge merchant ship, 40m in length. Fully laden it could hold 520 tons of cargo. The amphorae found held wine, oil & other goods like garum. Its discovery was also a breakthrough in marine archaeology

Ancient Olympic Games, quadrennial athletic event that was held in Olympia, Greece, from 776 BCE to about 393 CE. It was part of a religious festival that honoured Zeus, and the name Olympics was derived from Mount Olympus, home of the Greek gods.

The world's oldest condom, made of pig intestines in 1640 CE, discovered in Lund, Sweden. The owners manual advised men to dip it in unboiled milk before use to prevent diseases when engaging with prostitutes. It’s washable and reusable. On display at Tirolean County Museum in Austria.

Most of the historic ceramics that I've copied have a surface design. 18th C. British agateware is different, made of two clays wedged together. During the trimming process the marbling is revealed. I love how this one turned out! Sherds were recovered in St. Augustine, Florida .
#maniarchaeology 🏺

Best bird photos from 2023.
When word got out that rare scarlet-chested parrots had been seen in big numbers at Gluepot, birders (including me) flocked to see them. I had searched for these birds for years. This is a female scarlet-chested parrot feeding on cottony saltbush.
🪶 #birds

A Little Blue Heron and some White Ibises doing some afternoon fishing.

Ibis Fact: White Ibis nests can have nests from ground to as high up as 50ft 

L.B. Heron Fact: These Herons have white feathers that transition to its blue color over the first year of their lives/

#FloridaWildlife #Birds

Great egrets: masters of the art of striking a pose. With a slow, sassy strut, they wade through the waters in pursuit of their aquatic prey. Showcasing their prowess as ambush predators, these birds are striking in more than one way! Nature’s choreography unfolds in these feathered maestros.

Here's a great blue heron (I believe?) taking off from a pond in suburban Kansas City. The kids around the pond were in awe of the it flying away! Good reminder that urban wildlife is so important for connecting kids with their environment. 

🦉

This bird chowing down on a peanut is a brown thrasher. I suspect it may be the same one from last year, since they normally prefer thickets and shy away from houses. This one prefers the marsh and backyards.
📷📸🪶

A great part of Sayulita, Mexico: there's one patch of the Pacific Ocean that apparently is filled with the dumbest fish. Over and over brown pelicans speared into the water, shooting out their heads at the last moment like a snake striking to snatch fish from the water. #Mexico #Birds #Birding

Two nēnēs at WWT Slimbridge! Native to Hawai'i, and the rarest goose in the world, at one point numbering only 30 birds due to hunting and introduced predators like mongoose, pigs, and cats. Decades of conservation efforts have helped reintroduce the nēnē and native numbers are over 3,800 today 😊🪶

One of the exciting things about this time of year is the return of the nuthatches! They are in our  area all year, but we don’t see them nearly as often in the summer. This red-breasted Nuthatch was foraging on the tree trunks near sunset!🪶

One of my favorite photos from Kaua'i was of this Apapane feeding in an ohia tree, the signature Hawaiian native forest tree and main food source for these honeycreepers. Apapane are endemic to Hawaii and are found on most of the larger islands 🪶📷

Cassin’s Finch brings #BirdGlamour to conifer & quaking aspen forests in western North America! Similar to the House Finch but with a pointier crown, Cassin’s Finches are excellent mimics and add other species’ songs to theirs. They’re also a bit salty: they love salt and visit mineral deposits. 🐡🧪🐦

THE LILAC-BREASTFED ROLLER
 
Is a beautifully colored African bird from the roller family, famous for its acrobatic aerial displays during the breeding season. It is a Native of Southern & Eastern Africa, It prefers open woodland and savanna,& it is usually absent from treeless places.#Blacksky.

No Shelter But The Stars by Virginia Black, for a story that primarily takes place on a barren rock or in space, this novel is so viscerally lush with emotional depth! Even the most spartan passages hit so that you feel it with your whole chest. Just a hugely satisfying read.

One of my favorite quick salads is greens (baby romaine here) chicken (vegan for me), and blue cheese stuffed olives packed in olive oil. Make a simple quick vinaigrette with the oil from the olives jar and lemon juice. It’s so simple and delicious.

made what's probably my favorite Bryant Terry recipe, an ital stew with roasted root veggies, a ton of roasted garlic, and kidney beans pre-cooked in ginger and kombu. I don't make it that often cuz it's kind of a lift, but it's soooo delicious 😋🍽️

Went for an upscale steak house vibe for dinner tonight cooking for friends. Caprese salad…salad; double cut bone-in rib eye with purple and white asparagus in a lemon-basil vinaigrette, smashed garlic Yukon gold potatoes, and miso-butter onions. Burnt some bread but otherwise a success.

Just made us a nice lil brunch. Cooked onion and zucchini in leftover bacon grease, added Penzey’s Arizona Dreaming spice blend, a can of black beans, and some spicy fire-roasted salsa. Cracked eggs on top and covered to cook them through, topped with cheese and cilantro and served over chips. 🍜

granny dinner: craving made reality by 5pm! shredded seitan cooked in a guajillo sauce, on a toasted brioche with avocado, pickled onions, and roasted garlic aioli, with roasted root veggies on the side 😋🍽️🍜

Had some pals over yesterday for EDH Boxing League brunch and made stuffed French toast filled with talegio cheese, prosciutto, and a fig and mustard spread. Also made home fried potatoes w/onions and garlic and Luger’s bacon. Served here with Calabrian chile maple syrup.

todays goblin lunch is what i’m calling the “eggchilada bowl”— two soft boiled eggs & two fried bean n cheese corn tortilla tacos topped with salsa & tapatío . the yolks & salsas become the sauce for the tacos which then kinda taste like rich enchiladas. nom nom

I’m so excited to finally try this! Got all my sous vide gear set up (which was super easy). Now, the waiting game as my steaks rest in a nice, warm bath for 2 hours. Then, slap a nice sear on them and see how they taste.

Have you tried sous vide? Is it really that much better?

Shimi’s simple sticky sweet rice pudding in a rice cooker. Half a cup of Arborio rice. Saffron, Cardamom pods, Cinnamon. Nutmeg, 2 cups of coconut milk. Soy Yoghurt at serving. Add banana and pear to garnish.

I made salted salmon onigiri and pickled watermelon rind! Perfect for a hot day like this: the salmon took 6 minutes in the toaster oven, the rice cooker generates almost no heat, and the watermelon isn’t cooked at all.

My first attempt at making Spezzatino di Manzo. A braised beef dish that is served over polenta and moly goodness is it rich! Lots of mushrooms, slowly stewed chuck roast, and creamy polenta. It makes me so happy.

Daffodil bulbs planted in compacted soil vs bulbs in normal soil. 🌱

The compacted one is under a sycamore tree with good branches for climbing next to a freshman dorm, and the other one is 20 feet away without good climbing branches.

I love this dahlia bloom (Cen has been working on developing different varieties and it's fascinating).

Planted a Little Lemon clematis in a tall pit along a sunny hog wire fence. 🌱 I’ve tried a few annual vibes here without good results, so I’m at peace with the fact that this is a non-vining clematis (learned after planting).

I succumbed to Black Friday and got myself more Greenstalk self-watering planters. Highly recommended if you are short on space or have mobility issues. I grew a ton of tomatoes, beans and herbs in mine and want to expand.

🌱  Behind the tomatoes there is a hardy strawberry in full sun. Its two siblings are in the raised bed but I kept this one here and it grows a ripe strawberry every few days.
Very hardy plant, tasty sweet fruit.

I am selecting plants for the next years, this strawberry is here to stay.

While they love to grow in limestone, the Carnation doesn’t like the overly hot Summers of Florida. If you want to enjoy these blooms through Spring, best to plant in Fall. Small bushy plants that do not like wet conditions, be sure to monitor moisture levels during wet season!

These are the berries of trees growing along the edge of the marsh adjacent to my property.

Google identifies these as Red Chokeberries, but I’m not 100% convinced. They’re 20ft tall slender trees and make only a handful of berries each year. 🌱📸📷

Belle de Jour floribunda rose looking fabulous after overnight rain. A lovely apricot fragrance, she is full of blooms & will continue flowering until the first frost. Rose of the Year, 2021, bred by Maison Delbard.

Garden report 🌱
It’s either pouring rain or 100 degree heat index here in Nash. The basil went from beautiful to black overnight, squash bugs finally got the cucumbers, jalapeños are tiny, tomatillos huge but scarce. Tomatoes are surprisingly fine but must ripen indoors?
Mostly growing mosquitos

My nasturtiums, which had done nothing for the most part since I first planted them in May, are suddenly going wild now that it's nearly November. Climate change, man. Everything is weird now. (Bonus Harvey in this pic because it's his morning meditations spot.) 🌱

Golden Tōtara has been allowed to run out a bit on most lower branches.
Started to see thickening of the trunk recently.
Will continue to let vigour build it up for couple years. Not much else needed. Once structure is at desired scale will focus on foliage refinement.

A state university system shouldn’t need to exploit the labor of the skilled professionals who are doing the core business (teaching & research) of the system. Prioritizing paying those professionals a fair wage should be a given.

It's funny (but not really) that racists try to make DEI into an issue of hiring the "wrong" person instead of the "right" one. A ton of DEI work involves trying to retain people.

I think most of us who've worked for awhile have seen all kinds of qualified marginalized people pushed or burned out.

The Texas A&M Board of Regents directed the system's office of general counsel to conduct a “complete and thorough investigation” into what happened with Kathleen McElroy's job offer, and —this part is key — pledged to release the results to the public.

The board also authorized settlement talks.

The closure of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion programs at the University of #Texas at Austin have been an unmitigated catastrophe for students and faculty, according to this new OpEd from two college professors:

An attitude I hear all over higher ed — that helping students understand skills gained in college turns liberal arts into voc-tech — is elitist on so many levels, from disdain for other routes to higher ed to the presumption that students have the luxury to just figure it out.

I have often wondered why bullying is so common in academia. Is it because it is so dependent on personal relationships,  patronage and reputation. Or because it is normalized in its everyday functioning? Or because it is deeply hierarchical under its liberal facade? I think all of it is true.

It is beginning to look like the probable result of Texas cracking down on college DEI programs and such is opening themselves up to a series of slam dunk lawsuits that cost them money and end up publicizing all of the emails and texts of the boards of regents and university executives

1/ When we complain about the lack of racial diversity in academia every “well actually guy” says it’s a pipeline problem and implies minorities are to blame. Now men are less likely to get degrees and it’s not a pipeline problem but discrimination…

A cold rocky world languishes in the feeble glow of a brown dwarf, itself part of a distant binary pair whose partner can just be seen as a small magenta speck just over its limb. Perhaps a view like this exists around the real-life brown dwarf binary Luhman-16, the closest brown dwarfs to Sol.

An Indian spacecraft became the first to land on the rugged south pole of the moon on Wednesday. This is a great achievement. It's one of the signs that India is becoming a world power.
Nevertheless it's painful that 1.3 billion Indians still live in poverty. It would be better to tackle this first.

This gigantic supercluster is the largest known structure in the universe, it consists of 830 separate galaxies which are inside of 4 connected galaxy clusters. The supercluster is known as the “BOSS”, the entire structure stretches approximately 1 billion light years across.

In time for your bedtime space tweet, NASA drops a new image from the Webb Telescope. "NGC 604 rolling out the red carpet for more than 200 new stars"
NGC 604 is approximately 2.7M light-years away from Earth within the Triangulum Galaxy. Ignore Hollywood, here’s where stars are born.

Messier 82- Some galaxies are especially good at generating bright new stars and have so much galactic dust and gas that they create stars  at a furious rate. M82 is also called the “Cigar galaxy” due the red glow of Hydrogen “smoke” emanating from its galactic core.Imaged from my Ottawa backyard.

This time last year saw one of SOFIA's last flights take place at the Edwards Airshow. SOFIA, or the Stratospheric Observatory For Infrared Astronomy first started scientific flights in 2014, averaging roughly one hundred such flights per year.

For years, black holes were just an odd but valid solution to Einstein’s general relativity equations. The *math* predicted them, but nobody knew if they were real. Now, of course, we know that they’re everywhere, including the huge one at the center of our galaxy. Physics is so cool.

Thinking again about how scientists have evidence for a probable concentration of mass millions of times bigger than our galaxy pulling everything toward it and decided to name it “The Great Attractor” instead of something cool like “The Devouring Sky.”

a likely RIP to Voyager 1, the first human made object to reach interstellar space, an invaluable scientific tool, and the camera for one of the most poignant photos ever taken. the entirety of all of the lives of everyone we’ve ever known or will ever know, all on 0.12 pixels

Unity, Epic Games, EA, and other game companies: We're gonna repeatedly onboard thousands of new employees and then axe them all a year later

Pro-Tip: 90% of the time you ask "why is thing bad in game?" The answer is "the gamedevs were not given enough time, poorly treated, underpaid, or all three."

The great games you want are directly tied to the working conditions of the developers making them.

The games industry isn't broken, it's working as intended. That distinction is important because if it were broken, people in power at the top would want to fix it.

But they don't because it benefits them this way, which means that we need to force the change from the bottom.

Video game studios - and all corporations, realistically - should not be completely opaque black boxes that only allow us to see good things about them on their marketing teams’ terms. And journalists are not beholden to supporting companies’ attempts to “surprise and delight customers.”

In shocking news, game developers report that they, in fact, cannot do everything at once all the time forever, that some things just get triaged away when audience sentiment forces it, and that it is in fact not possible to "just make everyone happy". 

Community outraged.

As a Gamer American, there’s nothing that excites me more than when people who make games lose their jobs. Anyone who makes games is standing between me and getting all the good games with good graphics I want, which the corporations will give to me if I’m good. I am very smart.

Mother Dreyer's Small Potatoes:

Toss 1 or 2 cans small white potatoes in a Pyrex dish with oil, garlic salt, and paprika, then bake for an hour, uncovered, at 350°, turning them occasionally.

This is my new favourite way to make potatoes - steam until soft, then partially crush in a baking tray and drizzle with olive oil and a pinch of salt, then roast for about 90 min. You can also pour over some vinegar before roasting for salt and vinegar potatoes which goes really well with fish 😋

It’s now been a year since my apartment’s gym complex fully opened back up after having limited access restrictions for COVID. 

Turns out that COVID and associated limited access restrictions were not why I wasn’t going to the gym.

The last four months are the first time since the gyms reopened post-pandemic that I’ve noticed sustained growth in size and strength. Out of the 18-month fog of a relationship slowly ending, recommitted to the gym, and seeing major differences. 

I have my time and energy back, and I love it.

I often get asked "how" I do things.

"How do you get up to lift so early?"

"How do you work out when you don't feel motivated?"

There's no how, no secret, no trick. I can't give you advice that will make you get up and get to the gym. It's JUST a matter of... doing it.
